pairs.quijote_half: so as to find in the darkness an excuse for his blundering.
pairs.genji_source:
Some say that the name of Hikaru the Shining One was given to him in
admiration by the Korean fortune-teller.
pairs.quijote_source:
The night was darkish, though Sancho
would have been glad had it been quite dark, so as to find in the
darkness an excuse for his blundering.

pairs.display: There were not many people about in the palace to find all this, Sancho?
pairs.genji_half: There were not many people about in the palace
pairs.quijote_half: to find all this, Sancho?
pairs.genji_source:
There were not many people about in the palace and Genji’s rooms
seemed even quieter than usual.
pairs.quijote_source:
And where do
you expect to find all this, Sancho?

pairs.display: He begged so eagerly that but do you know her house, Sancho?
pairs.genji_half: He begged so eagerly that
pairs.quijote_half: but do you know her house, Sancho?
pairs.genji_source: He begged so eagerly that Genji let him examine the drawers.
pairs.quijote_source:
That’s all very well, but do you know
her house, Sancho?

pairs.display: More often he was wrong and Genji, amused by his puzzled air, and won’t put up with liberties from anybody.
pairs.genji_half: More often he was wrong and Genji, amused by his puzzled air,
pairs.quijote_half: and won’t put up with liberties from anybody.
pairs.genji_source:
More
often he was wrong and Genji, amused by his puzzled air, said very
little but generally managed to lead him astray.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don’t you trust to that, Sancho, for the Manchegan folk are as
hot-tempered as they are honest, and won’t put up with liberties from
anybody.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō handed over to them the decision of Genji’s question, he returned full speed to seek his master, and found him sighing and uttering a thousand passionate lamentations.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō handed over to them the decision of Genji’s question,
pairs.quijote_half: he returned full speed to seek his master, and found him sighing and uttering a thousand passionate lamentations.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō
handed over to them the decision of Genji’s question, and in the
discussion which followed many unflattering things were said.
pairs.quijote_source:
To be brief, the instant Sancho saw the peasant girls, he returned full
speed to seek his master, and found him sighing and uttering a thousand
passionate lamentations.

pairs.display: He thought as he said this of his own sister, Princess Aoi; but was disappointed and neighbour of Sancho Panza’s, a lively, feather-headed fellow, offered himself as his squire.
pairs.genji_half: He thought as he said this of his own sister, Princess Aoi; but was disappointed
pairs.quijote_half: and neighbour of Sancho Panza’s, a lively, feather-headed fellow, offered himself as his squire.
pairs.genji_source:
He thought as he said
this of his own sister, Princess Aoi; but was disappointed and piqued
to discover that Genji, whose comments he awaited, was fast asleep.
pairs.quijote_source:
Carrasco
undertook the task, and Tom Cecial, a gossip and neighbour of Sancho
Panza’s, a lively, feather-headed fellow, offered himself as his squire.

pairs.display: So saying, he came having extracted a laugh from his master’s profound melancholy, and excited fresh amazement in Don Diego.
pairs.genji_half: So saying, he came
pairs.quijote_half: having extracted a laugh from his master’s profound melancholy, and excited fresh amazement in Don Diego.
pairs.genji_source: So saying, he came and sat a little closer to them, and Genji woke up.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho went back and regained his pack-saddle, having extracted a laugh
from his master’s profound melancholy, and excited fresh amazement in Don
Diego.

pairs.display: Still less do princes like you came up to his master, to whom there fell a terrific and desperate adventure.
pairs.genji_half: Still less do princes like you
pairs.quijote_half: came up to his master, to whom there fell a terrific and desperate adventure.
pairs.genji_source:
Still less do princes like you and Genji
require so huge a stock of intellect and erudition for your support!
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, hearing himself called, quitted
the shepherds, and, prodding Dapple vigorously, came up to his master, to
whom there fell a terrific and desperate adventure.

pairs.display: Here he stopped, but Genji and the rest, wishing to hear the end of the story, and protection of the enchanters with sorrow enough in his heart.
pairs.genji_half: Here he stopped, but Genji and the rest, wishing to hear the end of the story,
pairs.quijote_half: and protection of the enchanters with sorrow enough in his heart.
pairs.genji_source:
Here he stopped, but Genji and the rest, wishing to hear the end
of the story, cried out that for their part they found her a most
interesting woman.
pairs.quijote_source:
As Sancho said this, he tied the beasts, leaving them to the care and
protection of the enchanters with sorrow enough in his heart.

pairs.display: After this long residence at the Palace Genji knew he would took to his heels as hard as he could and strove in vain to mount a tall oak.
pairs.genji_half: After this long residence at the Palace Genji knew he would
pairs.quijote_half: took to his heels as hard as he could and strove in vain to mount a tall oak.
pairs.genji_source:
After this long residence at the
Palace Genji knew he would be expected at the Great Hall and set out
at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho alone, deserting Dapple at the sight of
the mighty beast, took to his heels as hard as he could and strove in
vain to mount a tall oak.

pairs.display: This Ki no Kami was where in a spacious chamber there was a table laid out with royal magnificence.
pairs.genji_half: This Ki no Kami was
pairs.quijote_half: where in a spacious chamber there was a table laid out with royal magnificence.
pairs.genji_source:
This Ki no Kami was one of Genji’s gentlemen in
waiting.
pairs.quijote_source:
The history says that from the justice court they carried Sancho to a
sumptuous palace, where in a spacious chamber there was a table laid out
with royal magnificence.

pairs.display: But the subsequent conversation of the ladies soon showed that they for there was only that seat placed, and no more than one cover laid.
pairs.genji_half: But the subsequent conversation of the ladies soon showed that they
pairs.quijote_half: for there was only that seat placed, and no more than one cover laid.
pairs.genji_source:
But the
subsequent conversation of the ladies soon showed that they knew
nothing of the matter at all, and Genji stopped listening.
pairs.quijote_source:
The music ceased, and Sancho
seated himself at the head of the table, for there was only that seat
placed, and no more than one cover laid.

pairs.display: This was all rather dull and Genji by a quotation from an old into his own hands or those of his secretary.
pairs.genji_half: This was all rather dull and Genji by a quotation from an old
pairs.quijote_half: into his own hands or those of his secretary.
pairs.genji_source:
This was all rather dull and Genji by a quotation from an old
folk-song hinted that he would like to meet Ki no Kami’s other guests.
pairs.quijote_source:
To Don
Sancho Panza, Governor of the Island of Barataria, into his own hands or
those of his secretary.

pairs.display: There were in the room several charming boys, sons of Ki no Kami, and are called The constitutions of the great governor Sancho Panza.
pairs.genji_half: There were in the room several charming boys, sons of Ki no Kami,
pairs.quijote_half: and are called The constitutions of the great governor Sancho Panza.
pairs.genji_source:
There were in the room several charming boys, sons of Ki no Kami, some
of whom Genji already knew as pages at the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
In a word, he made so many good rules that
to this day they are preserved there, and are called The constitutions of
the great governor Sancho Panza.

pairs.display: Her sleepy voice was so like the boy’s, turning a considerable distance out of the road.
pairs.genji_half: Her sleepy voice was so like the boy’s,
pairs.quijote_half: turning a considerable distance out of the road.
pairs.genji_source:
Her sleepy voice was so like the
boy’s, that Genji concluded this must be his sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho complied, and Ricote having spoken to the other pilgrims they
withdrew to the grove they saw, turning a considerable distance out of
the road.

pairs.display: When all was quiet again, made the sun halt in his course to behold them, and held all four in a strange silence.
pairs.genji_half: When all was quiet again,
pairs.quijote_half: made the sun halt in his course to behold them, and held all four in a strange silence.
pairs.genji_source:
When all was quiet again,
Genji slipped back the bolt and tried the door.
pairs.quijote_source:
Such was the spectacle that filled Sancho with amazement, fascinated Don
Quixote, made the sun halt in his course to behold them, and held all
four in a strange silence.

pairs.display: But if I should touch this fine gentleman ...,’ eager to see what would be the upshot of his vainglorious and extraordinary proposal.
pairs.genji_half: But if I should touch this fine gentleman ...,’
pairs.quijote_half: eager to see what would be the upshot of his vainglorious and extraordinary proposal.
pairs.genji_source:
But if I
should touch this fine gentleman ...,’ and all in a flutter she found
herself obediently following Genji to his room.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho followed on Dapple, together with all the members of
the pastoral gathering, eager to see what would be the upshot of his
vainglorious and extraordinary proposal.

pairs.display: She was no great beauty, Genji reflected, and Sancho asked the landlord what he had to give them for supper.
pairs.genji_half: She was no great beauty, Genji reflected,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho asked the landlord what he had to give them for supper.
pairs.genji_source:
She
was no great beauty, Genji reflected, and yet one could not say that
she was ugly.
pairs.quijote_source:
Supper-time came, and they repaired to their
room, and Sancho asked the landlord what he had to give them for supper.

pairs.display: He was not exactly handsome, but he had great charm and restored to him the treasures and jewels they had stripped off Dapple.
pairs.genji_half: He was not exactly handsome, but he had great charm
pairs.quijote_half: and restored to him the treasures and jewels they had stripped off Dapple.
pairs.genji_source:
He was not
exactly handsome, but he had great charm and (thought Genji) an air of
distinction.
pairs.quijote_source:
Roque on coming up asked Sancho if his men had returned and
restored to him the treasures and jewels they had stripped off Dapple.

pairs.display: The boy was quite content with this explanation, and accidents that had happened his master.
pairs.genji_half: The boy was quite content with this explanation,
pairs.quijote_half: and accidents that had happened his master.
pairs.genji_source:
The boy was quite content with this explanation, and admired Genji
more than ever.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the meantime
Sancho had recounted to them several of the adventures and accidents that
had happened his master.

pairs.display: He had already given a message to his sister, so as not to let him leave the house.
pairs.genji_half: He had already given a message to his sister,
pairs.quijote_half: so as not to let him leave the house.
pairs.genji_source:
He
had already given a message to his sister, in which Genji told her of
his plan.
pairs.quijote_source:
Orders were left with the
servants to entertain Sancho so as not to let him leave the house.

pairs.display: The boy was terribly sorry for Genji and did not feel sleepy at all, of the generous disposition of the lacquey Tosilos.
pairs.genji_half: The boy was terribly sorry for Genji and did not feel sleepy at all,
pairs.quijote_half: of the generous disposition of the lacquey Tosilos.
pairs.genji_source:
The boy was terribly
sorry for Genji and did not feel sleepy at all, but he was afraid
people would think his continual excursions very strange.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho came up and spoke in
high praise of the generous disposition of the lacquey Tosilos.

pairs.display: He was well content to find himself lying by this handsome young Prince’s side, and the sun smote Sancho on the eyes with his beams.
pairs.genji_half: He was well content to find himself lying by this handsome young Prince’s side,
pairs.quijote_half: and the sun smote Sancho on the eyes with his beams.
pairs.genji_source:
He was well content to find himself lying by
this handsome young Prince’s side, and Genji, we must record, found
the boy no bad substitute for his ungracious sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now daylight came, and the sun smote Sancho on the eyes with his
beams.

pairs.display: We learn later that Genji courted this as the object they had in view was now attained.
pairs.genji_half: We learn later that Genji courted this
pairs.quijote_half: as the object they had in view was now attained.
pairs.genji_source:
We learn later that Genji courted this lady in vain from his
seventeenth year onward.
pairs.quijote_source:
Rhadamanthus bade Sancho put away his wrath, as the object they had in
view was now attained.

pairs.display: This worried the boy, when wilt thou begin the scourging?
pairs.genji_half: This worried the boy,
pairs.quijote_half: when wilt thou begin the scourging?
pairs.genji_source:
This worried the boy, but he was inwardly flattered at the
confidence which Genji placed in him.
pairs.quijote_source:
But look here, Sancho; when wilt thou begin
the scourging?

pairs.display: They entered unobserved at a side-gate, and Sancho asked one of them what their quarrel was about.
pairs.genji_half: They entered unobserved at a side-gate,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho asked one of them what their quarrel was about.
pairs.genji_source:
They entered unobserved at a side-gate, and here he bade Genji
descend.
pairs.quijote_source:
The two boys who had been quarrelling came over to look at the hare, and
Sancho asked one of them what their quarrel was about.

pairs.display: Longing to catch sight of her, even though she were with a companion, he asked them what it was those cloths covered.
pairs.genji_half: Longing to catch sight of her, even though she were with a companion,
pairs.quijote_half: he asked them what it was those cloths covered.
pairs.genji_source:
Longing to
catch sight of her, even though she were with a companion, Genji stole
from his hiding-place, and crept through a gap in the curtains.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote approached the diners, and, saluting them
courteously first, he asked them what it was those cloths covered.

pairs.display: The best thing to be done under the circumstances was to wait and Don Quixote had to give way and comply.
pairs.genji_half: The best thing to be done under the circumstances was to wait
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Quixote had to give way and comply.
pairs.genji_source:
The
best thing to be done under the circumstances was to wait till no one
was about, and then lead Genji straight to her.
pairs.quijote_source:
The gay
shepherd offered him his services and begged that he would accompany him
to their tents, and Don Quixote had to give way and comply.

pairs.display: Soon however, he got up and spread a screen as though to protect him from the light, the expedient of the great Don Quixote’s expedition to Barbary should be adopted.
pairs.genji_half: Soon however, he got up and spread a screen as though to protect him from the light,
pairs.quijote_half: the expedient of the great Don Quixote’s expedition to Barbary should be adopted.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon however, he got
up and spread a screen as though to protect him from the light, and
under its shadow Genji slipped softly into the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Antonio then said that if the renegade did not prove successful, the
expedient of the great Don Quixote’s expedition to Barbary should be
adopted.

pairs.display: The princely scent which still clung and the bachelor Samson Carrasco busy with their breviaries.
pairs.genji_half: The princely scent which still clung
pairs.quijote_half: and the bachelor Samson Carrasco busy with their breviaries.
pairs.genji_source:
The
princely scent which still clung to Genji’s person reached the bed.
pairs.quijote_source:
They then went on, and upon the green at the entrance of the town
they came upon the curate and the bachelor Samson Carrasco busy with
their breviaries.

pairs.display: Genji felt very uncomfortable, and making no answer on the supposed Mimbu’s behalf, and his two friends, with Don Quixote and Sancho, went to the galleys.
pairs.genji_half: Genji felt very uncomfortable, and making no answer on the supposed Mimbu’s behalf,
pairs.quijote_half: and his two friends, with Don Quixote and Sancho, went to the galleys.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji felt very uncomfortable, and making no answer on
the supposed Mimbu’s behalf, he stood in the shadow at the end of the
corridor, hiding himself as best he could.
pairs.quijote_source:
To resume; that afternoon their host Don Antonio Moreno and his two
friends, with Don Quixote and Sancho, went to the galleys.

pairs.display: So narrow an escape made Genji wonder more and very happy in another.
pairs.genji_half: So narrow an escape made Genji wonder more
pairs.quijote_half: and very happy in another.
pairs.genji_source:
So narrow an escape made Genji wonder more than ever whether the whole
thing was worth while.
pairs.quijote_source:
The vanquished and afflicted Don Quixote went along very downcast in one
respect and very happy in another.

pairs.display: For a while they rested, but Genji could not sleep, like a gentleman of his rank, was very likely perfumed as Sancho said.
pairs.genji_half: For a while they rested, but Genji could not sleep,
pairs.quijote_half: like a gentleman of his rank, was very likely perfumed as Sancho said.
pairs.genji_source:
For a while they rested, but Genji could not sleep, and at dawn he
sent in haste for his ink-stone.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was here speaking of Don
Fernando, who, like a gentleman of his rank, was very likely perfumed as
Sancho said.

pairs.display: Certain that Genji himself would be very much shocked, and begged him to come down to where they stood.
pairs.genji_half: Certain that Genji himself would be very much shocked,
pairs.quijote_half: and begged him to come down to where they stood.
pairs.genji_source:
Certain that Genji himself would be very much shocked, they
exchanged uneasy glances.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote called
aloud to him and begged him to come down to where they stood.

pairs.display: It occurred at once to Genji that it was and to their life of beasts, and so ended the adventure of the enchanted bark.
pairs.genji_half: It occurred at once to Genji that it was
pairs.quijote_half: and to their life of beasts, and so ended the adventure of the enchanted bark.
pairs.genji_source:
It occurred at once to Genji that it was one of
these Courtiers who had written the poem.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote and Sancho returned to their
beasts, and to their life of beasts, and so ended the adventure of the
enchanted bark.

pairs.display: Meanwhile, lighted only by a dim torch, so exhilarated at finding himself now dubbed a knight, that his joy was like to burst his horse-girths.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile, lighted only by a dim torch,
pairs.quijote_half: so exhilarated at finding himself now dubbed a knight, that his joy was like to burst his horse-girths.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile, lighted only by a dim torch, Genji quietly left his nurse’s
home.
pairs.quijote_source:
Day was dawning when Don Quixote quitted the inn, so happy, so gay, so
exhilarated at finding himself now dubbed a knight, that his joy was like
to burst his horse-girths.

pairs.display: Her face was calm, but she looked very unhappy, who, as afterwards appeared, were some Toledo traders, on their way to buy silk at Murcia.
pairs.genji_half: Her face was calm, but she looked very unhappy,
pairs.quijote_half: who, as afterwards appeared, were some Toledo traders, on their way to buy silk at Murcia.
pairs.genji_source:
Her face was calm, but she looked very
unhappy, and I noticed that some of her women were secretly weeping.’
Genji was more curious than ever.
pairs.quijote_source:
After he
had gone about two miles Don Quixote perceived a large party of people,
who, as afterwards appeared, were some Toledo traders, on their way to
buy silk at Murcia.

pairs.display: After being many times roused Genji at last came out of Rokujō’s room, the unlucky Don Quixote and Sancho alone were unable to move from where they were.
pairs.genji_half: After being many times roused Genji at last came out of Rokujō’s room,
pairs.quijote_half: the unlucky Don Quixote and Sancho alone were unable to move from where they were.
pairs.genji_source:
After being many
times roused Genji at last came out of Rokujō’s room, looking very
cross and sleepy.
pairs.quijote_source:
The innkeeper
retreated to his room, the carrier to his pack-saddles, the lass to her
crib; the unlucky Don Quixote and Sancho alone were unable to move from
where they were.

pairs.display: So many lovely flowers were growing in the borders that Genji halted Cardenio was in a similar state of mind, and Luscinda’s thoughts ran in the same direction.
pairs.genji_half: So many lovely flowers were growing in the borders that Genji halted
pairs.quijote_half: Cardenio was in a similar state of mind, and Luscinda’s thoughts ran in the same direction.
pairs.genji_source:
So many lovely flowers were
growing in the borders that Genji halted for a while to enjoy them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea was unable to persuade herself
that her present happiness was not all a dream; Cardenio was in a similar
state of mind, and Luscinda’s thoughts ran in the same direction.

pairs.display: One who had a favourite daughter would ask so much so that he began to abandon the notion he had taken up about his being crazy.
pairs.genji_half: One who had a favourite daughter would ask
pairs.quijote_half: so much so that he began to abandon the notion he had taken up about his being crazy.
pairs.genji_source:
One who had a
favourite daughter would ask for nothing better than to make her
Genji’s handmaiden.
pairs.quijote_source:
He of the green gaban was filled with astonishment at Don Quixote’s
argument, so much so that he began to abandon the notion he had taken up
about his being crazy.

pairs.display: What a queer place to be lying in! thought Genji, fancying himself the most valorous knight-errant of the age in the world because of his late victory.
pairs.genji_half: What a queer place to be lying in! thought Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: fancying himself the most valorous knight-errant of the age in the world because of his late victory.
pairs.genji_source:
What a queer place to be lying in! thought
Genji, as he gazed round the garret, so different from any room he had
ever known before.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote pursued his journey in the high spirits, satisfaction, and
self-complacency already described, fancying himself the most valorous
knight-errant of the age in the world because of his late victory.

pairs.display: While he waited for the steward to come out Genji noticed Sancho, and he of the green gaban went theirs.
pairs.genji_half: While he waited for the steward to come out Genji noticed
pairs.quijote_half: Sancho, and he of the green gaban went theirs.
pairs.genji_source:
While he
waited for the steward to come out Genji noticed that the gates were
crumbling away; dense shinobu-grass grew around them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cart went its way, and Don Quixote, Sancho, and he of the green gaban
went theirs.

pairs.display: The twanging of the bow though they did not understand one half of what he meant by them.
pairs.genji_half: The twanging of the bow
pairs.quijote_half: though they did not understand one half of what he meant by them.
pairs.genji_source:
The twanging of the bow reminded Genji of the
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The men were filled with wonder, as well at the figure as at the words of
Don Quixote, though they did not understand one half of what he meant by
them.

pairs.display: It was of course contrary to etiquette that he should serve Genji himself but so many thrust themselves between them that he could not avenge him.
pairs.genji_half: It was of course contrary to etiquette that he should serve Genji himself
pairs.quijote_half: but so many thrust themselves between them that he could not avenge him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was of course
contrary to etiquette that he should serve Genji himself and he
hesitated in embarrassment, not venturing even to ascend the dais.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, seeing him so
roughly handled, attacked the man who had struck him lance in hand, but
so many thrust themselves between them that he could not avenge him.

pairs.display: Thinking that Genji would not wish to move the body himself, having once seen them, shut his eyes so as not to see them again.
pairs.genji_half: Thinking that Genji would not wish to move the body himself,
pairs.quijote_half: having once seen them, shut his eyes so as not to see them again.
pairs.genji_source:
Thinking that Genji
would not wish to move the body himself, he wrapt it in a rush-mat and
carried it towards the carriage.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leading it were two
hideous demons, also clad in buckram, with countenances so frightful that
Sancho, having once seen them, shut his eyes so as not to see them again.

pairs.display: But Genji in his sickness and despair saw none of this, and Don Alvaro dined together.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji in his sickness and despair saw none of this,
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Alvaro dined together.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji in his sickness and despair saw none of this,
and suddenly waking from the stupor into which he had fallen found
that they had arrived.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this dinner-time arrived, and Don Quixote and Don Alvaro dined
together.

pairs.display: A senior priest, son of the aged nun, now began to recite the Scriptures in an impressive voice, begged him to give him what was left in the pigskin, which was no small quantity.
pairs.genji_half: A senior priest, son of the aged nun, now began to recite the Scriptures in an impressive voice,
pairs.quijote_half: begged him to give him what was left in the pigskin, which was no small quantity.
pairs.genji_source:
A senior priest, son of the aged nun, now began to recite the
Scriptures in an impressive voice, and Genji as he listened felt the
tears come into his eyes.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho Panza, who also regarded the amendment of his master as
miraculous, begged him to give him what was left in the pigskin, which
was no small quantity.

pairs.display: Hearing of his father’s distress and anxiety, and four pages came forward to present him with water for his hands, which Sancho received with great dignity.
pairs.genji_half: Hearing of his father’s distress and anxiety,
pairs.quijote_half: and four pages came forward to present him with water for his hands, which Sancho received with great dignity.
pairs.genji_source:
Hearing of his
father’s distress and anxiety, Genji strove hard to reassure him by
pretending to some slight respite or improvement.
pairs.quijote_source:
The clarions sounded as Sancho entered the room,
and four pages came forward to present him with water for his hands,
which Sancho received with great dignity.

pairs.display: To Genji even the din of the cloth-beaters’ mallets had become dear through recollection, and eager to serve his lord and lady he set off very willingly for Sancho’s village.
pairs.genji_half: To Genji even the din of the cloth-beaters’ mallets had become dear through recollection,
pairs.quijote_half: and eager to serve his lord and lady he set off very willingly for Sancho’s village.
pairs.genji_source:
To Genji even the din of the cloth-beaters’ mallets had become dear
through recollection, and as he lay in bed he repeated those verses of
Po Chü-i.

_
pairs.quijote_source:
Now the history says this page was very sharp and quick-witted; and eager
to serve his lord and lady he set off very willingly for Sancho’s
village.

pairs.display: But after all Shōshō would probably not take that so very hard, and gave thanks to God at the same time that his master had not found out what was the matter.
pairs.genji_half: But after all Shōshō would probably not take that so very hard,
pairs.quijote_half: and gave thanks to God at the same time that his master had not found out what was the matter.
pairs.genji_source:
But
after all Shōshō would probably not take that so very hard, Genji had
vanity enough to think.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho held his tongue, and gave him a cloth, and gave thanks to God at
the same time that his master had not found out what was the matter.

pairs.display: (For Genji, try as he might, and persuade him to speak to Luscinda’s father.
pairs.genji_half: (For Genji, try as he might,
pairs.quijote_half: and persuade him to speak to Luscinda’s father.
pairs.genji_source: (For Genji, try as he might, could not hide his tears.)
pairs.quijote_source:
To all this Don Fernando
answered that he would take it upon himself to speak to my father, and
persuade him to speak to Luscinda’s father.

pairs.display: By the time this was over, the sun had risen, and provoke him to make him come out.
pairs.genji_half: By the time this was over, the sun had risen,
pairs.quijote_half: and provoke him to make him come out.
pairs.genji_source:
By the time
this was over, the sun had risen, and Genji went a little way outside
the cave and looked around him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing this, Don Quixote ordered the keeper to take a
stick to him and provoke him to make him come out.

pairs.display: The nun raised her head as she spoke and all who heard him, were kept hanging upon his lips.
pairs.genji_half: The nun raised her head as she spoke
pairs.quijote_half: and all who heard him, were kept hanging upon his lips.
pairs.genji_source:
The nun raised her head as she
spoke and Genji fancied that there was some resemblance between her
and the child.
pairs.quijote_source:
Such were the
drolleries of Sancho that all the servants of the house, and all who
heard him, were kept hanging upon his lips.

pairs.display: Even Genji, peeping at the scene from a distance, and when they asked who was there, Sancho Panza made answer that it was he.
pairs.genji_half: Even Genji, peeping at the scene from a distance,
pairs.quijote_half: and when they asked who was there, Sancho Panza made answer that it was he.
pairs.genji_source:
Even Genji,
peeping at the scene from a distance, found himself becoming quite
distressed.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this moment there came a knocking at the door, and when they asked who
was there, Sancho Panza made answer that it was he.

pairs.display: I have just heard that Prince Genji has come who uttered them as though with immense pain he drew them up from his very bowels.
pairs.genji_half: I have just heard that Prince Genji has come
pairs.quijote_half: who uttered them as though with immense pain he drew them up from his very bowels.
pairs.genji_source:
I have just heard that Prince Genji has come
to the hermit yonder to be cured of an ague.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cousin and Sancho Panza listened with deep attention to the words of
Don Quixote, who uttered them as though with immense pain he drew them up
from his very bowels.

pairs.display: But you shall hear all about it....’ Before the old priest had time and come to close quarters with him, when he hoped to hew him in pieces.
pairs.genji_half: But you shall hear all about it....’ Before the old priest had time
pairs.quijote_half: and come to close quarters with him, when he hoped to hew him in pieces.
pairs.genji_source:
But you shall hear all
about it....’

Before the old priest had time to leave the house Genji was on his way
back to the hermit’s cave.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote merely
observed him steadily, longing for him to leap from the cart and come to
close quarters with him, when he hoped to hew him in pieces.

pairs.display: And all the while the priest was and Sancho’s, for Don Quixote’s fall did not suffer him to take the road sooner.
pairs.genji_half: And all the while the priest was
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho’s, for Don Quixote’s fall did not suffer him to take the road sooner.
pairs.genji_source:
And all the while the priest
was speaking Genji thought of his own wickedness.
pairs.quijote_source:
The day for Don Antonio’s departure came; and two days later that for Don
Quixote’s and Sancho’s, for Don Quixote’s fall did not suffer him to take
the road sooner.

pairs.display: How much the more so Prince Genji, and though they were not very good, they were far better than those he put off.
pairs.genji_half: How much the more so Prince Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and though they were not very good, they were far better than those he put off.
pairs.genji_source:
How much the more so
Prince Genji, as he lay sleepless on his bed, continually
planning and counter-planning!
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio had now put on
the clothes which Dorothea was wearing when they found her, and though
they were not very good, they were far better than those he put off.

pairs.display: The nun at once perceived that the poem referred to her grandchild and supposed that Genji, but Sancho took his departure in such a flurry that he never missed them.
pairs.genji_half: The nun at once perceived that the poem referred to her grandchild and supposed that Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: but Sancho took his departure in such a flurry that he never missed them.
pairs.genji_source:
The nun at once perceived that the poem
referred to her grandchild and supposed that Genji, having been
wrongly informed about her age, was intending to make love to
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is true that the innkeeper detained
his alforjas in payment of what was owing to him, but Sancho took his
departure in such a flurry that he never missed them.

pairs.display: Several of Genji’s friends now arrived to congratulate him upon his recovery, from which he came back much relieved and more eager than ever to put his squire’s scheme into execution.
pairs.genji_half: Several of Genji’s friends now arrived to congratulate him upon his recovery,
pairs.quijote_half: from which he came back much relieved and more eager than ever to put his squire’s scheme into execution.
pairs.genji_source:
Several of Genji’s friends now arrived to
congratulate him upon his recovery, among them a messenger from the
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so saying, accompanied by Sancho, he withdrew to
a retired spot, from which he came back much relieved and more eager than
ever to put his squire’s scheme into execution.

pairs.display: He begged to be excused and niece took him in and undressed him and laid him in his old bed.
pairs.genji_half: He begged to be excused
pairs.quijote_half: and niece took him in and undressed him and laid him in his old bed.
pairs.genji_source:
He
begged to be excused from accompanying Genji on his way.
pairs.quijote_source:
While this conversation passed between Sancho Panza and his wife, Don
Quixote’s housekeeper and niece took him in and undressed him and laid
him in his old bed.

pairs.display: Genji had sent to the Capital and raving, and slashing and cutting all round, as wide awake as if he had never slept.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had sent to the Capital
pairs.quijote_half: and raving, and slashing and cutting all round, as wide awake as if he had never slept.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
had sent to the Capital for gifts with which to repay his reception in
the mountain.
pairs.quijote_source:
When they reached Don Quixote he was already out of bed, and was still
shouting and raving, and slashing and cutting all round, as wide awake as
if he had never slept.

pairs.display: He asked many questions about the hermit’s magical powers, knight-errant taking with him an esquire mounted on ass-back, but no instance occurred to his memory.
pairs.genji_half: He asked many questions about the hermit’s magical powers,
pairs.quijote_half: knight-errant taking with him an esquire mounted on ass-back, but no instance occurred to his memory.
pairs.genji_source:
He
asked many questions about the hermit’s magical powers, to all of
which Genji replied in great detail.
pairs.quijote_source:
About the ass, Don Quixote hesitated a little, trying
whether he could call to mind any knight-errant taking with him an
esquire mounted on ass-back, but no instance occurred to his memory.

pairs.display: Aoi’s quarters had, in anticipation of Genji’s coming, Dorothea was not as he fancied the queen Micomicona, of whom he expected such great favours.
pairs.genji_half: Aoi’s quarters had, in anticipation of Genji’s coming,
pairs.quijote_half: Dorothea was not as he fancied the queen Micomicona, of whom he expected such great favours.
pairs.genji_source:
Aoi’s quarters had, in anticipation of Genji’s coming, just been put
thoroughly to rights.
pairs.quijote_source:
Even
Sancho Panza was weeping; though afterwards he said he only wept because
he saw that Dorothea was not as he fancied the queen Micomicona, of whom
he expected such great favours.

pairs.display: He repeated all that Genji had told him to say and that he means to publish them in hopes of Don Quixote’s third sally.
pairs.genji_half: He repeated all that Genji had told him to say
pairs.quijote_half: and that he means to publish them in hopes of Don Quixote’s third sally.
pairs.genji_source:
He repeated all that Genji had told
him to say and added a great deal of general information about his
master.
pairs.quijote_source:
We have been informed that at the cost of
many sleepless nights and much toil he has succeeded, and that he means
to publish them in hopes of Don Quixote’s third sally.

pairs.display: But at the end of it all Shōnagon was just as puzzled as everyone else had been to account who, not finding them where he had left them, was calling aloud to them.
pairs.genji_half: But at the end of it all Shōnagon was just as puzzled as everyone else had been to account
pairs.quijote_half: who, not finding them where he had left them, was calling aloud to them.
pairs.genji_source:
But at the end of it all Shōnagon was just as puzzled as
everyone else had been to account for Genji’s interest in a child so
ridiculously young.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this moment they heard a shout, and recognised it as coming from
Sancho Panza, who, not finding them where he had left them, was calling
aloud to them.

pairs.display: The sight of the Emperor’s grief and was soon out of reach.
pairs.genji_half: The sight of the Emperor’s grief
pairs.quijote_half: and was soon out of reach.
pairs.genji_source:
The sight of the Emperor’s grief and anxiety moved Genji’s
pity.
pairs.quijote_source: So saying, he gave Rocinante the spur and was soon out of reach.

pairs.display: And indeed, as Genji knew, it was not without good cause though he does not deserve it, for he was the main cause of all this thrashing.
pairs.genji_half: And indeed, as Genji knew, it was not without good cause
pairs.quijote_half: though he does not deserve it, for he was the main cause of all this thrashing.
pairs.genji_source:
And indeed, as Genji knew, it was not without good cause
that she had suddenly fallen into this fit of apprehension and
remorse.
pairs.quijote_source:
See if your worship can get up, and let us help Rocinante,
though he does not deserve it, for he was the main cause of all this
thrashing.

pairs.display: When the man announced that Prince Genji had sent him for news Was wont his wandering master to attend.
pairs.genji_half: When the man announced that Prince Genji had sent him for news
pairs.quijote_half: Was wont his wandering master to attend.
pairs.genji_source:
When the man announced that Prince Genji
had sent him for news and was himself waiting outside, great
excitement and consternation prevailed in the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
By Rocinante’s side this gentle squire
      Was wont his wandering master to attend.

pairs.display: This speech delighted Genji; but the gentlewomen of the household thought the child’s incursion painful began cutting such capers, that it flung the lady Dulcinea to the ground;
pairs.genji_half: This speech delighted Genji; but the gentlewomen of the household thought the child’s incursion painful
pairs.quijote_half: began cutting such capers, that it flung the lady Dulcinea to the ground;
pairs.genji_source:
This speech delighted Genji; but the gentlewomen of the
household thought the child’s incursion painful and unseemly, and
pretended not to hear her last remark.
pairs.quijote_source:
The she-ass, however, feeling the point more acutely than usual,
began cutting such capers, that it flung the lady Dulcinea to the ground;

pairs.display: In the tenth month the Emperor was to visit the Suzaku-in without dismounting from Dapple he followed in the footsteps of Rocinante.
pairs.genji_half: In the tenth month the Emperor was to visit the Suzaku-in
pairs.quijote_half: without dismounting from Dapple he followed in the footsteps of Rocinante.
pairs.genji_source:
In the tenth month the Emperor was to
visit the Suzaku-in for the Festival of Red Leaves.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so, vexed and dejected though not hungry,
without dismounting from Dapple he followed in the footsteps of
Rocinante.

pairs.display: This and more he wrote, and Genji, and to see them he spurred Rocinante and mounted the hill.
pairs.genji_half: This and more he wrote, and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and to see them he spurred Rocinante and mounted the hill.
pairs.genji_source:
This and more he wrote, and
Genji, reading the letter was filled with a bitter sense of life’s
briefness and futility.
pairs.quijote_source:
At
first he imagined some regiment of soldiers was passing that way, and to
see them he spurred Rocinante and mounted the hill.

pairs.display: Shōnagon, still mistrustful, sat quite and of one of the gentle families of El Toboso, which are many, ancient, and good.
pairs.genji_half: Shōnagon, still mistrustful, sat quite
pairs.quijote_half: and of one of the gentle families of El Toboso, which are many, ancient, and good.
pairs.genji_source: Shōnagon, still mistrustful, sat quite close to Genji all the while.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dulcinea is illustrious and well-born, and of
one of the gentle families of El Toboso, which are many, ancient, and
good.

pairs.display: Once she was in her father’s palace it would look very the fair, The peerless Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: Once she was in her father’s palace it would look very
pairs.quijote_half: the fair, The peerless Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
Once she was in her father’s
palace it would look very odd that Genji should fetch her away, even
if she came willingly.
pairs.quijote_source:
The sorrow-laden plaint of her, the fair,
The peerless Dulcinea del Toboso.

pairs.display: For Genji makeshift quarters were soon contrived That plump and lusty maid;
pairs.genji_half: For Genji makeshift quarters were soon contrived
pairs.quijote_half: That plump and lusty maid;
pairs.genji_source:
For Genji makeshift quarters were soon contrived
by letting down the side-wings of his screen-of-honour.
pairs.quijote_source:
O Dulcinea may be proud,
    That plump and lusty maid;

pairs.display: Murasaki was still asleep, they cannot let her enjoy my incomparable constancy in peace!
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki was still asleep,
pairs.quijote_half: they cannot let her enjoy my incomparable constancy in peace!
pairs.genji_source: Murasaki was still asleep, lying wrapped in Genji’s own coat.
pairs.quijote_source:
O that the peerless
Dulcinea should be so unfortunate that they cannot let her enjoy my
incomparable constancy in peace!

pairs.display: For two or three days Genji did not go to the Palace, and high-bred, and all others are ill-favoured, foolish, light, and low-born.
pairs.genji_half: For two or three days Genji did not go to the Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: and high-bred, and all others are ill-favoured, foolish, light, and low-born.
pairs.genji_source:
For two or three days Genji did not go to the Palace, but spent all
his time amusing the little girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
For me Dulcinea alone is beautiful, wise,
virtuous, graceful, and high-bred, and all others are ill-favoured,
foolish, light, and low-born.

pairs.display: She was a young person of very lively disposition and with half a hundred of wool hanging to each of his fetlocks.
pairs.genji_half: She was a young person of very lively disposition
pairs.quijote_half: and with half a hundred of wool hanging to each of his fetlocks.
pairs.genji_source:
She was a young person of
very lively disposition and Genji often made use of her services.
pairs.quijote_source:
The horse was a manifest
Frieslander, broad-backed and flea-bitten, and with half a hundred of
wool hanging to each of his fetlocks.

pairs.display: She happened one day when she was talking with Genji to mention a certain princess, but upon a tall mule of easy pace and handsomely caparisoned.
pairs.genji_half: She happened one day when she was talking with Genji to mention a certain princess,
pairs.quijote_half: but upon a tall mule of easy pace and handsomely caparisoned.
pairs.genji_source:
She happened one day when she was talking with Genji to mention a
certain princess, daughter of the late Prince Hitachi.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don
Quixote was mounted, not on Rocinante, but upon a tall mule of easy pace
and handsomely caparisoned.

pairs.display: It was indeed on the night after the full moon, just like one whose heart was pierced with grief at his defeat and his separation from Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_half: It was indeed on the night after the full moon,
pairs.quijote_half: just like one whose heart was pierced with grief at his defeat and his separation from Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_source:
It was indeed on the night after the full moon, in just such a veiled
light as Genji had spoken of, that they visited the Hitachi palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
He accompanied each verse with many sighs and not a few tears, just like
one whose heart was pierced with grief at his defeat and his separation
from Dulcinea.

pairs.display: True, she was not a great performer; but the instrument was a particularly fine one the bounds which the Christian religion we profess has assigned to us.
pairs.genji_half: True, she was not a great performer; but the instrument was a particularly fine one
pairs.quijote_half: the bounds which the Christian religion we profess has assigned to us.
pairs.genji_source:
True, she was not
a great performer; but the instrument was a particularly fine one and
Genji found her playing by no means unpleasant to listen to.
pairs.quijote_source:
So that, O Sancho, in what we do we must not
overpass the bounds which the Christian religion we profess has assigned
to us.

pairs.display: She remembered that Genji had often been obliged to reproach her all clad in ivy and hemp stained green, and looking so natural that they nearly terrified Sancho.
pairs.genji_half: She remembered that Genji had often been obliged to reproach her
pairs.quijote_half: all clad in ivy and hemp stained green, and looking so natural that they nearly terrified Sancho.
pairs.genji_source:
She remembered that Genji had often been obliged to reproach her for
her reckless flirtations, and blushing made no reply.
pairs.quijote_source:
In front of them all came a wooden castle drawn by four wild
men, all clad in ivy and hemp stained green, and looking so natural that
they nearly terrified Sancho.

pairs.display: This aroused Chūjō’s curiosity and, despite the fact that he too had a secret appointment that night, and what he was afraid of.
pairs.genji_half: This aroused Chūjō’s curiosity and, despite the fact that he too had a secret appointment that night,
pairs.quijote_half: and what he was afraid of.
pairs.genji_source:
This aroused
Chūjō’s curiosity and, despite the fact that he too had a secret
appointment that night, he decided first to follow Genji and discover
what was afoot.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote did so, and
asked him what had happened to him, and what he was afraid of.

pairs.display: Both of them got into Genji’s carriage and the moon having vanished behind a cloud, and the barber remained nearly a month without seeing him, lest they should recall or bring back to his recollection what had taken place.
pairs.genji_half: Both of them got into Genji’s carriage and the moon having vanished behind a cloud,
pairs.quijote_half: and the barber remained nearly a month without seeing him, lest they should recall or bring back to his recollection what had taken place.
pairs.genji_source:
Both of them got into Genji’s carriage and the moon having
vanished behind a cloud, beguiled the way to the Great Hall by playing
a duet upon their flutes.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cide Hamete Benengeli, in the Second Part of this history, and third
sally of Don Quixote, says that the curate and the barber remained nearly
a month without seeing him, lest they should recall or bring back to his
recollection what had taken place.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō who had been her who showed them to the duchess and they were both amazed afresh at the madness and wit of Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō who had been her
pairs.quijote_half: who showed them to the duchess and they were both amazed afresh at the madness and wit of Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō who had
been her lover because of her infatuation for Genji
pairs.quijote_source:
They
had scarcely, however, been given to him when he let them drop, and they
fell into the hands of the duke, who showed them to the duchess and they
were both amazed afresh at the madness and wit of Don Quixote.

pairs.display: But the same desperate shyness still possessed her and leave him to sleep, for that was what he needed most.
pairs.genji_half: But the same desperate shyness still possessed her
pairs.quijote_half: and leave him to sleep, for that was what he needed most.
pairs.genji_source:
But the same desperate shyness
still possessed her and Genji’s letters she would not even read.
pairs.quijote_source:
They put a host of questions to Don Quixote, but his only answer to all
was--give him something to eat, and leave him to sleep, for that was what
he needed most.

pairs.display: The place where Genji was waiting was well screened from view since thou hast set thy tongue going against the peerless Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_half: The place where Genji was waiting was well screened from view
pairs.quijote_half: since thou hast set thy tongue going against the peerless Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_source:
The
place where Genji was waiting was well screened from view and he had
no difficulty in creeping unobserved into the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don’t fancy it, impious scoundrel, for
that beyond a doubt thou art, since thou hast set thy tongue going
against the peerless Dulcinea.

pairs.display: She said the words in such a way that Genji was completely taken and duennas gathered round him, waiting in profound silence to hear what he would say.
pairs.genji_half: She said the words in such a way that Genji was completely taken
pairs.quijote_half: and duennas gathered round him, waiting in profound silence to hear what he would say.
pairs.genji_source:
She said the words in such a way that Genji was completely
taken in and thought it was the princess who had thus readily answered
his poem.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho shrugged his
shoulders, obeyed, and sat down, and all the duchess’s damsels and
duennas gathered round him, waiting in profound silence to hear what he
would say.

pairs.display: He did indeed find her in the last extremity of bashfulness the champaign Of Aranjuez, and Montiel’s famous plain: On Rocinante oft a weary ride.
pairs.genji_half: He did indeed find her in the last extremity of bashfulness
pairs.quijote_half: the champaign Of Aranjuez, and Montiel’s famous plain: On Rocinante oft a weary ride.
pairs.genji_source:
He did indeed find
her in the last extremity of bashfulness and embarrassment, but under
the circumstances that, thought Genji, was natural.
pairs.quijote_source:
For her, Toboso’s queen, from side to side
  He traversed the grim sierra, the champaign
  Of Aranjuez, and Montiel’s famous plain:
On Rocinante oft a weary ride.

pairs.display: Seeing that Chūjō was in a hurry Genji said and the bachelor the shepherd Carrascon, and the curate the shepherd Curambro, and Sancho Panza the shepherd Pancino.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing that Chūjō was in a hurry Genji said
pairs.quijote_half: and the bachelor the shepherd Carrascon, and the curate the shepherd Curambro, and Sancho Panza the shepherd Pancino.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing that Chūjō was in a hurry Genji said that he
would go with him to the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote replied that he himself was to be called the shepherd
Quixotize and the bachelor the shepherd Carrascon, and the curate the
shepherd Curambro, and Sancho Panza the shepherd Pancino.

pairs.display: But it was not quite so large but answered him never a word, such was the distress and anguish the poor beast found himself in.
pairs.genji_half: But it was not quite so large
pairs.quijote_half: but answered him never a word, such was the distress and anguish the poor beast found himself in.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was not quite so large
and was (to Genji’s comfort) at least to some small degree inhabited.
pairs.quijote_source:
In this strain did Sancho bewail himself, and his ass listened to him,
but answered him never a word, such was the distress and anguish the poor
beast found himself in.

pairs.display: Although it was still dark the light of the snow enabled the ancient gentlewomen who others upon the life he was about to lead in his enforced retirement.
pairs.genji_half: Although it was still dark the light of the snow enabled the ancient gentlewomen who
pairs.quijote_half: others upon the life he was about to lead in his enforced retirement.
pairs.genji_source:
Although it was
still dark the light of the snow enabled the ancient gentlewomen who
had now returned to the room to see the freshness and beauty of
Genji’s face.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some of them
turned upon the disenchantment of Dulcinea, others upon the life he was
about to lead in his enforced retirement.

pairs.display: As usual she seemed quite devoid of conversation as fine as fine can be.
pairs.genji_half: As usual she seemed quite devoid of conversation
pairs.quijote_half: as fine as fine can be.
pairs.genji_source:
As usual she seemed quite devoid of
conversation and her silence ended by depriving Genji also of the
power of speech.
pairs.quijote_source:
Perhaps behind some bush we shall find the lady Dulcinea
disenchanted, as fine as fine can be.

pairs.display: Taking pity on them one of Genji’s servants went to their assistance the duke with boar-spear did the same; but the duchess would have gone in front of them all had not the duke prevented her.
pairs.genji_half: Taking pity on them one of Genji’s servants went to their assistance
pairs.quijote_half: the duke with boar-spear did the same; but the duchess would have gone in front of them all had not the duke prevented her.
pairs.genji_source:
Taking pity on them one of Genji’s
servants went to their assistance and quickly opened it.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as he saw him Don Quixote, bracing his shield on his arm,
and drawing his sword, advanced to meet him; the duke with boar-spear did
the same; but the duchess would have gone in front of them all had not
the duke prevented her.

pairs.display: When Myōbu presented Genji’s reply, opinion whether they might open the letter that had come for the governor, which she suspected must be very good.
pairs.genji_half: When Myōbu presented Genji’s reply,
pairs.quijote_half: opinion whether they might open the letter that had come for the governor, which she suspected must be very good.
pairs.genji_source:
When
Myōbu presented Genji’s reply, the ladies of the Hitachi Palace
gathered round her to admire it.
pairs.quijote_source:
All were greatly amused by Teresa Panza’s letter, but particularly the
duke and duchess; and the duchess asked Don Quixote’s opinion whether
they might open the letter that had come for the governor, which she
suspected must be very good.

pairs.display: The princess’s ancient gentlewomen realized that Genji did not approve of their mistress’s taste in colours till Sancho, perceiving that daybreak was coming on apace, very cautiously untied Rocinante and tied up his breeches.
pairs.genji_half: The princess’s ancient gentlewomen realized that Genji did not approve of their mistress’s taste in colours
pairs.quijote_half: till Sancho, perceiving that daybreak was coming on apace, very cautiously untied Rocinante and tied up his breeches.
pairs.genji_source:
The princess’s ancient gentlewomen
realized that Genji did not approve of their mistress’s taste in
colours and wished to give her a lesson.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this and other talk of the same sort master and man passed the
night, till Sancho, perceiving that daybreak was coming on apace, very
cautiously untied Rocinante and tied up his breeches.

pairs.display: The Emperor too could not bear that Fujitsubo should miss the spectacle, it would be necessary to devise or discover some other way of getting him home.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor too could not bear that Fujitsubo should miss the spectacle,
pairs.quijote_half: it would be necessary to devise or discover some other way of getting him home.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor
too could not bear that Fujitsubo should miss the spectacle, and he
decided to hold a grand rehearsal in the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
But now, the curate said, that the lady Dorothea’s good
fortune prevented her from proceeding with their purpose, it would be
necessary to devise or discover some other way of getting him home.

pairs.display: The Emperor insisted upon treating Genji’s performance at the rehearsal as a kind of miracle or religious portent, on his head, his buckler on his arm, and leaning on his staff or pike.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor insisted upon treating Genji’s performance at the rehearsal as a kind of miracle or religious portent,
pairs.quijote_half: on his head, his buckler on his arm, and leaning on his staff or pike.
pairs.genji_source:
The
Emperor insisted upon treating Genji’s performance at the rehearsal as
a kind of miracle or religious portent, and ordered special services
to be read in every temple.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this moment Don Quixote came out in full panoply, with Mambrino’s
helmet, all dinted as it was, on his head, his buckler on his arm, and
leaning on his staff or pike.

pairs.display: She continued to use the somewhat outlying western wing which had now been put into excellent order, in order that it may tell who the Knight of the Mirrors and his long-nosed squire were.
pairs.genji_half: She continued to use the somewhat outlying western wing which had now been put into excellent order,
pairs.quijote_half: in order that it may tell who the Knight of the Mirrors and his long-nosed squire were.
pairs.genji_source:
She continued to
use the somewhat outlying western wing which had now been put into
excellent order, and here Genji constantly came to see her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote and Sancho resumed their journey to
Saragossa, and on it the history leaves them in order that it may tell
who the Knight of the Mirrors and his long-nosed squire were.

pairs.display: He was naturally very much gratified, and Don Quixote placed himself at her service with an abundance of well-chosen and polished phrases.
pairs.genji_half: He was naturally very much gratified,
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Quixote placed himself at her service with an abundance of well-chosen and polished phrases.
pairs.genji_source:
He was naturally very much gratified, not realizing that Genji
had now, in a sense, become his son-in-law.
pairs.quijote_source:
The lady, whose name was Dona Christina, received him with every sign of
good-will and great courtesy, and Don Quixote placed himself at her
service with an abundance of well-chosen and polished phrases.

pairs.display: It was with bitterness that Genji remembered how long not by the hand of God, but by the claws of a cat, mishaps incidental to knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_half: It was with bitterness that Genji remembered how long
pairs.quijote_half: not by the hand of God, but by the claws of a cat, mishaps incidental to knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_source:
It was with
bitterness that Genji remembered how long ago the Emperor had brought
her to play with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Exceedingly moody and dejected was the sorely wounded Don Quixote, with
his face bandaged and marked, not by the hand of God, but by the claws of
a cat, mishaps incidental to knight-errantry.

pairs.display: There was a doll’s kitchen only three feet high but fitted out with all the proper utensils, as they knew very well there was nothing in that inn fit for one of his sort.
pairs.genji_half: There was a doll’s kitchen only three feet high but fitted out with all the proper utensils,
pairs.quijote_half: as they knew very well there was nothing in that inn fit for one of his sort.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a doll’s kitchen only three feet high but fitted out with
all the proper utensils, and a whole collection of little houses which
Genji had made for her.
pairs.quijote_source:
The two gentlemen pressed Don Quixote to come into their room and have
supper with them, as they knew very well there was nothing in that inn
fit for one of his sort.

pairs.display: Her admonishment had the effect of making Murasaki thus reminded of his old quarters, stepped out so briskly that he hardly seemed to tread the earth.
pairs.genji_half: Her admonishment had the effect of making Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: thus reminded of his old quarters, stepped out so briskly that he hardly seemed to tread the earth.
pairs.genji_source:
Her
admonishment had the effect of making Murasaki for the first time
aware that Genji was to be her husband.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this object he
turned his horse’s head towards his village, and Rocinante, thus reminded
of his old quarters, stepped out so briskly that he hardly seemed to
tread the earth.

pairs.display: To Genji all these pretensions naturally seemed somewhat exaggerated as he wanted to take them home for a token and memento of that unexampled adventure.
pairs.genji_half: To Genji all these pretensions naturally seemed somewhat exaggerated
pairs.quijote_half: as he wanted to take them home for a token and memento of that unexampled adventure.
pairs.genji_source:
To Genji all these pretensions naturally seemed somewhat
exaggerated and his failure to make allowances for them increased her
hostility.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho begged the duke to let them
leave him the robe and mitre; as he wanted to take them home for a token
and memento of that unexampled adventure.

pairs.display: This one is not of much account save that the workmanship of it is rather unusual,’ Don Fernando at Cardenio, Cardenio at Luscinda, and Luscinda at Cardenio.
pairs.genji_half: This one is not of much account save that the workmanship of it is rather unusual,’
pairs.quijote_half: Don Fernando at Cardenio, Cardenio at Luscinda, and Luscinda at Cardenio.
pairs.genji_source:
This one is not of much account save
that the workmanship of it is rather unusual,’ and despite Genji’s
protests he insisted upon buckling it round him.
pairs.quijote_source:
They gazed at one another without speaking, Dorothea at Don Fernando, Don
Fernando at Cardenio, Cardenio at Luscinda, and Luscinda at Cardenio.

pairs.display: What did it matter if Genji and counterpane of the bed, tongue-tied, and unable to offer her any civility.
pairs.genji_half: What did it matter if Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and counterpane of the bed, tongue-tied, and unable to offer her any civility.
pairs.genji_source:
What did it matter
if Genji was rather irregular in his visits?
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, disconcerted and in confusion at her
appearance, huddled himself up and well-nigh covered himself altogether
with the sheets and counterpane of the bed, tongue-tied, and unable to
offer her any civility.

pairs.display: The delay made Genji more certain than ever of his own responsibility years he would have found enough to observe and wonder at in his mode of life.
pairs.genji_half: The delay made Genji more certain than ever of his own responsibility
pairs.quijote_half: years he would have found enough to observe and wonder at in his mode of life.
pairs.genji_source:
The delay made Genji more certain than ever of his own responsibility
and he arranged secretly for prayers on her behalf to be said in all
the great temples.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote passed three days and three nights with Roque, and had he
passed three hundred years he would have found enough to observe and
wonder at in his mode of life.

pairs.display: The Emperor was impatient to see Fujitsubo’s child and where the rocks and trees afforded a cool and grateful shade.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor was impatient to see Fujitsubo’s child
pairs.quijote_half: and where the rocks and trees afforded a cool and grateful shade.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor was impatient to see Fujitsubo’s child and so too (though
he was forced to conceal his interest in the matter) was Genji
himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho pushed on into the glens of the Sierra, leaving them in one
through which there flowed a little gentle rivulet, and where the rocks
and trees afforded a cool and grateful shade.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo meanwhile, fearing lest Genji’s continual visits should attract notice, whereat Sancho was in high delight, as he had never seen any all his life.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo meanwhile, fearing lest Genji’s continual visits should attract notice,
pairs.quijote_half: whereat Sancho was in high delight, as he had never seen any all his life.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo meanwhile, fearing lest
Genji’s continual visits should attract notice, began to suspect that
Ōmyōbu was secretly encouraging him and no longer felt the same
affection for her.
pairs.quijote_source:
That same day Don Antonio arranged to take him to see the galleys that
lay at the beach, whereat Sancho was in high delight, as he had never
seen any all his life.

pairs.display: Its extraordinary resemblance to Genji was not remarked upon by the Emperor who sorely shaken by his fall; and he, limping, advanced as best he could to kneel before the noble pair.
pairs.genji_half: Its extraordinary resemblance to Genji was not remarked upon by the Emperor who
pairs.quijote_half: sorely shaken by his fall; and he, limping, advanced as best he could to kneel before the noble pair.
pairs.genji_source:
Its extraordinary resemblance to
Genji was not remarked upon by the Emperor who had an idea that
handsome children were all very much alike at that age.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke ordered his huntsmen to go to the help of knight and squire, and
they raised Don Quixote, sorely shaken by his fall; and he, limping,
advanced as best he could to kneel before the noble pair.

pairs.display: For Genji himself he had always had so great a partiality that, without departing even in smallest particular from the style in which, as the stories tell us, they used to treat the knights of old.
pairs.genji_half: For Genji himself he had always had so great a partiality that,
pairs.quijote_half: without departing even in smallest particular from the style in which, as the stories tell us, they used to treat the knights of old.
pairs.genji_source:
For Genji himself he had always had so great a
partiality that, had it not been for popular opposition, he would
certainly have installed him as Heir Apparent.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke gave fresh orders
with respect to treating Don Quixote as a knight-errant, without
departing even in smallest particular from the style in which, as the
stories tell us, they used to treat the knights of old.

pairs.display: A succession of violent emotions—terror, shame, pride and love—passed through Genji’s breast one leading to Don Quixote’s village, the other the road Don Alvaro was to follow.
pairs.genji_half: A succession of violent emotions—terror, shame, pride and love—passed through Genji’s breast
pairs.quijote_half: one leading to Don Quixote’s village, the other the road Don Alvaro was to follow.
pairs.genji_source:
A succession of violent emotions—terror, shame, pride and
love—passed through Genji’s breast while these words were being
spoken, and were reflected in his rapidly changing colour.
pairs.quijote_source:
Evening came, they set out from the village, and after about half a
league two roads branched off, one leading to Don Quixote’s village, the
other the road Don Alvaro was to follow.

pairs.display: Stroking the lovely hair that had fallen forward across her lap who kept him from going to his master’s assistance.
pairs.genji_half: Stroking the lovely hair that had fallen forward across her lap
pairs.quijote_half: who kept him from going to his master’s assistance.
pairs.genji_source:
Stroking the
lovely hair that had fallen forward across her lap Genji asked her if
she missed him when he was away.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho alone was frantic, for he could not free himself from the
grasp of one of the canon’s servants, who kept him from going to his
master’s assistance.

pairs.display: The Emperor too had heard that there was someone living with Genji and leading her to a seat beside herself, requested her to remove her veil.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor too had heard that there was someone living with Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and leading her to a seat beside herself, requested her to remove her veil.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor too had heard that there was someone living with Genji and
thought it a great pity.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea took the Moorish
lady by the hand and leading her to a seat beside herself, requested her
to remove her veil.

pairs.display: The Emperor began to fear that his marriage with Aoi had proved a very unhappy one how like a child Dona Clara spoke.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor began to fear that his marriage with Aoi had proved a very unhappy one
pairs.quijote_half: how like a child Dona Clara spoke.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor began to
fear that his marriage with Aoi had proved a very unhappy one and was
sorry that he had arranged it.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea could not help laughing to hear how like a child Dona Clara
spoke.

pairs.display: There were few among them whom Genji could not and most of those present saw they were tricked and made fools of.
pairs.genji_half: There were few among them whom Genji could not
pairs.quijote_half: and most of those present saw they were tricked and made fools of.
pairs.genji_source:
There were few among them
whom Genji could not by the slightest word or gesture have made his
own.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, the priest and
Camacho and most of those present saw they were tricked and made fools
of.

pairs.display: On the night when the new Empress was installed Genji, continuing her story, went on to say:
pairs.genji_half: On the night when the new Empress was installed Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: continuing her story, went on to say:
pairs.genji_source:
On the
night when the new Empress was installed Genji, as a Counsellor, was
among those who accompanied her to the Middle Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
She, however, without noticing the excitement of Cardenio, continuing her
story, went on to say:

pairs.display: It astonished Genji that despite her advancing years she and rained down upon him such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with blood as freely as his own.
pairs.genji_half: It astonished Genji that despite her advancing years she
pairs.quijote_half: and rained down upon him such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with blood as freely as his own.
pairs.genji_source:
It astonished Genji that despite
her advancing years she showed no sign of reforming her reckless and
fantastic behaviour.
pairs.quijote_source:
The canon and the curate, however, prevented him, but the barber so
contrived it that he got Don Quixote under him, and rained down upon him
such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with
blood as freely as his own.

pairs.display: Her new intrigue was of course carried on with absolute secrecy to be sure, still lay on his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.
pairs.genji_half: Her new intrigue was of course carried on with absolute secrecy
pairs.quijote_half: to be sure, still lay on his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.
pairs.genji_source:
Her new intrigue was of
course carried on with absolute secrecy and Genji knew nothing about
it.
pairs.quijote_source:
The officer came up, and finding them engaged in such a peaceful
conversation, stood amazed; though Don Quixote, to be sure, still lay on
his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.

pairs.display: Wondering how much longer Genji would take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word But hogsheads full--this doth he know-- Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow, And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: Wondering how much longer Genji would take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word
pairs.quijote_half: But hogsheads full--this doth he know-- Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow, And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
Wondering how much longer Genji would
take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word but putting on the most
ferocious air imaginable drew his sword from the scabbard.
pairs.quijote_source:
Why Love should like a spiteful foe
  Thus use him, he hath no idea,
But hogsheads full--this doth he know--
Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow,
    And all for distant Dulcinea
                       Del Toboso.

pairs.display: In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and asked him in a low voice as everything would be done exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.
pairs.genji_half: In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and asked him in a low voice
pairs.quijote_half: as everything would be done exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.
pairs.genji_source:
In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and
asked him in a low voice whether he had decided in future to be a
little more communicative about his affairs.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate did not forget to instruct Dorothea how to act, but she
said they might make their minds easy, as everything would be done
exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.

pairs.display: Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji that and he lay down at once, being sorely shaken and in want of sleep.
pairs.genji_half: Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji that
pairs.quijote_half: and he lay down at once, being sorely shaken and in want of sleep.
pairs.genji_source:
Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji
that even the other princes of the Blood Royal stood somewhat in awe
of him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote said he would, so they made up a tolerable one for
him in the same garret as before; and he lay down at once, being sorely
shaken and in want of sleep.

pairs.display: An empress was appointed with the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.
pairs.genji_half: An empress was appointed
pairs.quijote_half: with the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.
pairs.genji_source:
An empress
was appointed and Genji was raised to the rank of Counsellor.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was a renewal of offers of service and civilities, and then, with
the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their
departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.

pairs.display: It was of course Fujitsubo whom inasmuch as for a vanquished knight like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal palace.
pairs.genji_half: It was of course Fujitsubo whom
pairs.quijote_half: inasmuch as for a vanquished knight like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal palace.
pairs.genji_source: It was of course Fujitsubo whom the Emperor chose in this case.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged their permission
to take his departure that same day, inasmuch as for a vanquished knight
like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal
palace.

pairs.display: The reading of Genji’s verses was the canon, and the barber, who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.
pairs.genji_half: The reading of Genji’s verses was
pairs.quijote_half: the canon, and the barber, who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.
pairs.genji_source:
The reading of Genji’s
verses was continually interrupted by loud murmurs of applause.
pairs.quijote_source:
The knight-errant and the ill-errant squire kept up their conversation
till they reached the place where the curate, the canon, and the barber,
who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.

pairs.display: The moon had risen very bright and clear, and Genji, and declared Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such charms.
pairs.genji_half: The moon had risen very bright and clear, and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and declared Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such charms.
pairs.genji_source:
The moon had risen very bright and clear, and
Genji, heated with wine, could not bear to quit so lovely a scene.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
were all highly delighted with her grace, air, and beauty, and declared
Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such
charms.

pairs.display: After the banquet Kōkiden herself had filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.
pairs.genji_half: After the banquet Kōkiden herself had
pairs.quijote_half: filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.
pairs.genji_source:
After the banquet
Kōkiden herself had gone straight to the Emperor’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source:
A strange sight was this for Don Fernando and those that stood around,
filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.

pairs.display: She looked, thought Genji, Quixote he would have punished them for their impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.
pairs.genji_half: She looked, thought Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: Quixote he would have punished them for their impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.
pairs.genji_source:
She looked, thought Genji, as though many disquieting
reflections were crowding into her mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
It appeared afterwards that the duke had sworn that if they had not
washed him as they had Don Quixote he would have punished them for their
impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.

pairs.display: There was just time to exchange fans in token of their and that no one could tell whither she had gone.
