pairs.display: The poem did strike Genji as somewhat forward in tone, May thy falsehood to me Find its punishment in her, For in my land the just Often pays for the sinner.
pairs.genji_half: The poem did strike Genji as somewhat forward in tone,
pairs.quijote_half: May thy falsehood to me Find its punishment in her, For in my land the just Often pays for the sinner.
pairs.genji_source:
The poem did strike Genji as somewhat forward in tone, and
this surprised him.
pairs.quijote_source:
May no bowels of mercy
  To Sancho be granted,
And thy Dulcinea
  Be left still enchanted,
May thy falsehood to me
  Find its punishment in her,
For in my land the just
  Often pays for the sinner.

pairs.display: The Emperor having assumed an attitude of respectful attention, to all which the Judge gave such an attentive hearing that he never before had been so much of a hearer.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor having assumed an attitude of respectful attention,
pairs.quijote_half: to all which the Judge gave such an attentive hearing that he never before had been so much of a hearer.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor having
assumed an attitude of respectful attention, the apparition proceeded
to deliver a long discourse, part of which was concerned with Genji’s
present plight.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here the curate went on to relate briefly his brother’s adventure with
Zoraida; to all which the Judge gave such an attentive hearing that he
never before had been so much of a hearer.

pairs.display: The Emperor was very much frightened, and being in any case somewhat uneasy at Genji’s prolonged absence, and the other made off, ready to die with laughing, leaving him fastened in such a way that it was impossible for him to release himself.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor was very much frightened, and being in any case somewhat uneasy at Genji’s prolonged absence,
pairs.quijote_half: and the other made off, ready to die with laughing, leaving him fastened in such a way that it was impossible for him to release himself.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor was very much frightened, and being in
any case somewhat uneasy at Genji’s prolonged absence, he hastened to
communicate his dream to Kōkiden.
pairs.quijote_source:
But there was nobody now to listen to these words of Don Quixote’s, for
as soon as Maritornes had tied him she and the other made off, ready to
die with laughing, leaving him fastened in such a way that it was
impossible for him to release himself.

pairs.display: It seemed somehow to be accepted that and everything won his heart.
pairs.genji_half: It seemed somehow to be accepted that
pairs.quijote_half: and everything won his heart.
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed somehow to be accepted that for Genji to pay
a visit to the house on the hill was entirely out of the question.
pairs.quijote_source: Sancho observed all, contemplated all, and everything won his heart.

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi was convinced that and freedom from incontinence, that all the mares in the Cordova pastures would not lead him into an impropriety.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi was convinced that
pairs.quijote_half: and freedom from incontinence, that all the mares in the Cordova pastures would not lead him into an impropriety.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi was convinced that
Genji regarded her in just such a light.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho had not thought it worth while to hobble Rocinante, feeling sure,
from what he knew of his staidness and freedom from incontinence, that
all the mares in the Cordova pastures would not lead him into an
impropriety.

pairs.display: The old priest looked in his calendar, chose a lucky day, and peered between the legs of Rocinante to see if he could now discover what it was that caused him such fear and apprehension.
pairs.genji_half: The old priest looked in his calendar, chose a lucky day,
pairs.quijote_half: and peered between the legs of Rocinante to see if he could now discover what it was that caused him such fear and apprehension.
pairs.genji_source:
The old priest looked in his calendar,
chose a lucky day, and despite the misgivings of his wife began to
prepare the house on the hill for Genji’s visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho who never quitted his
side, stretched his neck as far as he could and peered between the legs
of Rocinante to see if he could now discover what it was that caused him
such fear and apprehension.

pairs.display: His host had provided him with a magnificent coach, leaning against the bars as silent and as patient as if he were a stone statue and not a man of flesh.
pairs.genji_half: His host had provided him with a magnificent coach,
pairs.quijote_half: leaning against the bars as silent and as patient as if he were a stone statue and not a man of flesh.
pairs.genji_source:
His
host had provided him with a magnificent coach, but the narrow lanes
would have made its use inconvenient and Genji preferred to go
on horseback.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote was seated in the cage, with his hands tied and his
feet stretched out, leaning against the bars as silent and as patient as
if he were a stone statue and not a man of flesh.

pairs.display: Why, wondered Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits, he was completely puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.
pairs.genji_half: Why, wondered Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits,
pairs.quijote_half: he was completely puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.
pairs.genji_source:
Why, wondered
Genji, was he, who took such pleasure in quiet and harmless pursuits,
doomed on every occasion to find himself involved in the most harrowing
and disastrous situations?
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote looked all along the road to El Toboso, and as
he could see nobody except the three peasant girls, he was completely
puzzled, and asked Sancho if it was outside the city he had left them.

pairs.display: Two days before his departure Genji visited the house and Sancho at that of a beech, for trees of this kind and others like them always have feet but no hands.
pairs.genji_half: Two days before his departure Genji visited the house
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho at that of a beech, for trees of this kind and others like them always have feet but no hands.
pairs.genji_source:
Two days before his departure Genji visited the house on the hill some
hours earlier than was his wont.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
then entered the grove, and Don Quixote settled himself at the foot of an
elm, and Sancho at that of a beech, for trees of this kind and others
like them always have feet but no hands.

pairs.display: After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.
pairs.genji_half: After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight
pairs.quijote_half: he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.
pairs.genji_source:
After this visit Genji’s first care was to perform the ceremonial Eight
Readings of the Lotus Sūtra in memory of his father the late Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as
Rocinante found himself free, though by nature he was not at all
mettlesome, he seemed to feel lively and began pawing--for as to
capering, begging his pardon, he knew not what it meant.

pairs.display: At last Lady Gosechi, who silently and unknown to all the world had been grieving bitterly at Genji’s exile, he had nothing for it but to stand upright or pull his hand off.
pairs.genji_half: At last Lady Gosechi, who silently and unknown to all the world had been grieving bitterly at Genji’s exile,
pairs.quijote_half: he had nothing for it but to stand upright or pull his hand off.
pairs.genji_source:
At last Lady Gosechi, who silently and unknown to all the world
had been grieving bitterly at Genji’s exile, was able to relieve her
feelings by taking action.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is true he
pulled it gently lest Rocinante should move, but try as he might to seat
himself in the saddle, he had nothing for it but to stand upright or pull
his hand off.

pairs.display: Since the night of his so vivid and disquieting dream, and beautiful burden he bears in his lord and lady.
pairs.genji_half: Since the night of his so vivid and disquieting dream,
pairs.quijote_half: and beautiful burden he bears in his lord and lady.
pairs.genji_source:
Since the night of his so vivid and disquieting dream, the late Emperor
had been constantly in Genji’s thoughts.
pairs.quijote_source:
You see, too, how the neighing of the horse shows his
satisfaction with the gallant and beautiful burden he bears in his lord
and lady.

pairs.display: The manner in which this function was attended received lay unable to stir for the present.
pairs.genji_half: The manner in which this function was attended
pairs.quijote_half: received lay unable to stir for the present.
pairs.genji_source:
The manner in which this function was attended showed that
Genji had fully regained his former ascendancy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Rocinante from the mere hard measure he had received lay unable to stir
for the present.

pairs.display: He was only eleven years old but was big for his age, but that three kerchiefs that were worth three cities were missing.
pairs.genji_half: He was only eleven years old but was big for his age,
pairs.quijote_half: but that three kerchiefs that were worth three cities were missing.
pairs.genji_source:
He was only eleven years old but was big
for his age, and it was already apparent that he was developing an
extraordinary resemblance to his guardian, Prince Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho said they had, but that three kerchiefs that were worth three
cities were missing.

pairs.display: Yūgiri, Genji’s son by Aoi, for the bed of Sancho Panza, who still slept, and crouching upon it made a ball of herself.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri, Genji’s son by Aoi,
pairs.quijote_half: for the bed of Sancho Panza, who still slept, and crouching upon it made a ball of herself.
pairs.genji_source: Yūgiri, Genji’s son by Aoi, was a fine little fellow.
pairs.quijote_source:
The wench,
seeing that her master was coming and knowing that his temper was
terrible, frightened and panic-stricken made for the bed of Sancho Panza,
who still slept, and crouching upon it made a ball of herself.

pairs.display: Her one consolation had been that by her death Aoi had been spared the torture which Genji’s disgrace and the pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation not inferior to the previous one.
pairs.genji_half: Her one consolation had been that by her death Aoi had been spared the torture which Genji’s disgrace
pairs.quijote_half: and the pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation not inferior to the previous one.
pairs.genji_source:
Her one consolation had been that
by her death Aoi had been spared the torture which Genji’s disgrace
and banishment would have inflicted upon her proud and fastidious
nature.
pairs.quijote_source:
The niece let him in, and his
master Don Quixote came forward to receive him with open arms, and the
pair shut themselves up in his room, where they had another conversation
not inferior to the previous one.

pairs.display: Genji continued to show her the same attentions as before his exile and the first thing he saw was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.
pairs.genji_half: Genji continued to show her the same attentions as before his exile
pairs.quijote_half: and the first thing he saw was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji continued to show her the same
attentions as before his exile and lost no opportunity of going over to
the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio also heard
Dorothea’s cry as she fell fainting, and imagining that it came from his
Luscinda burst forth in terror from the room, and the first thing he saw
was Don Fernando with Luscinda in his arms.

pairs.display: This was Genji’s first daughter, of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who, irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.
pairs.genji_half: This was Genji’s first daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who, irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.
pairs.genji_source: This was Genji’s first daughter, and he felt quite excited.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this Sancho awoke, and feeling this mass
almost on top of him fancied he had the nightmare and began to distribute
fisticuffs all round, of which a certain share fell upon Maritornes, who,
irritated by the pain and flinging modesty aside, paid back so many in
return to Sancho that she woke him up in spite of himself.

pairs.display: She had sometimes done service at the Palace opinion of it as the curate, he begged him to read it so that they might all hear it.
pairs.genji_half: She had sometimes done service at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: opinion of it as the curate, he begged him to read it so that they might all hear it.
pairs.genji_source:
She had sometimes done service at the Palace and this was not the first
time Genji had seen her.
pairs.quijote_source:
While they were talking Cardenio had taken up the novel and begun to read
it, and forming the same opinion of it as the curate, he begged him to
read it so that they might all hear it.

pairs.display: These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection comes victor over himself, which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone can desire.
pairs.genji_half: These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection
pairs.quijote_half: comes victor over himself, which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone can desire.
pairs.genji_source:
These fresh tokens of Genji’s affection and concern could not fail
somewhat to revive her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Open thine arms and receive, too, thy son Don Quixote,
who, if he comes vanquished by the arm of another, comes victor over
himself, which, as he himself has told me, is the greatest victory anyone
can desire.

pairs.display: The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching and treated him as a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.
pairs.genji_half: The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching
pairs.quijote_half: and treated him as a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.
pairs.genji_source:
The messenger duly arrived, bearing with him the most touching and
gratifying tokens of Genji’s anxiety for the welfare of his friends.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some of Don Antonio’s
friends dined with him that day, and all showed honour to Don Quixote and
treated him as a knight-errant, and he becoming puffed up and exalted in
consequence could not contain himself for satisfaction.

pairs.display: What a lot of nice people there and a graduate of Siguenza) as to which had been the better knight, Palmerin of England or Amadis of Gaul.
pairs.genji_half: What a lot of nice people there
pairs.quijote_half: and a graduate of Siguenza) as to which had been the better knight, Palmerin of England or Amadis of Gaul.
pairs.genji_source:
What a lot of nice people there seemed to be in the world,
thought Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Many an argument did he have with the curate of his village (a learned
man, and a graduate of Siguenza) as to which had been the better knight,
Palmerin of England or Amadis of Gaul.

pairs.display: Thus Genji, as a near neighbour, was constantly consulted by the Prince’s staff with a bearing of disdain, Is Dulcinea, she for whom in vain The great Don Quixote of La Mancha sighed.
pairs.genji_half: Thus Genji, as a near neighbour, was constantly consulted by the Prince’s staff
pairs.quijote_half: with a bearing of disdain, Is Dulcinea, she for whom in vain The great Don Quixote of La Mancha sighed.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus Genji, as a near neighbour, was constantly consulted by the
Prince’s staff and was often able to be of considerable assistance to
them.
pairs.quijote_source:
PANIAGUADO,
ACADEMICIAN OF ARGAMASILLA,
IN LAUDEM DULCINEAE DEL TOBOSO

SONNET

She, whose full features may be here descried,
  High-bosomed, with a bearing of disdain,
  Is Dulcinea, she for whom in vain
The great Don Quixote of La Mancha sighed.

pairs.display: Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself and bade Sancho get down from Dapple and tie both beasts securely to the trunk of a poplar or willow that stood there.
pairs.genji_half: Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself
pairs.quijote_half: and bade Sancho get down from Dapple and tie both beasts securely to the trunk of a poplar or willow that stood there.
pairs.genji_source:
Power was now pretty equally divided between Genji himself
and his father-in-law, the old Minister at the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote looked all round, and seeing
nobody, at once, without more ado, dismounted from Rocinante and bade
Sancho get down from Dapple and tie both beasts securely to the trunk of
a poplar or willow that stood there.

pairs.display: In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi, hitting on the secret of the trick, but all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of Dulcinea’s disenchantment.
pairs.genji_half: In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi,
pairs.quijote_half: hitting on the secret of the trick, but all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of Dulcinea’s disenchantment.
pairs.genji_source:
In the autumn Genji made his pilgrimage to the Shrine of Sumiyoshi,
where, as will be remembered, he had various vows to fulfil.
pairs.quijote_source:
Profound were Don Quixote’s reflections on the reply of the enchanted
head, not one of them, however, hitting on the secret of the trick, but
all concentrated on the promise, which he regarded as a certainty, of
Dulcinea’s disenchantment.

pairs.display: She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she arrived and smote him such a blow with it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.
pairs.genji_half: She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she arrived
pairs.quijote_half: and smote him such a blow with it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
She determined to renew the practice, and it so happened that she
arrived by boat at Sumiyoshi just as Genji’s magnificent procession
was passing along the shore.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of those, however, that stood near him, fancying he was mocking them,
lifted up a long staff he had in his hand and smote him such a blow with
it that Sancho dropped helpless to the ground.

pairs.display: Not a word concerning Genji’s visit to Sumiyoshi had reached her, lean yellow face half a league long, his armour of all sorts, and the solemnity of his deportment.
pairs.genji_half: Not a word concerning Genji’s visit to Sumiyoshi had reached her,
pairs.quijote_half: lean yellow face half a league long, his armour of all sorts, and the solemnity of his deportment.
pairs.genji_source:
Not a word concerning Genji’s visit to
Sumiyoshi had reached her, and turning to some one who was standing
near she asked what procession this might be.
pairs.quijote_source:
The strange figure he presented filled Don
Fernando and the rest with amazement as they contemplated his lean yellow
face half a league long, his armour of all sorts, and the solemnity of
his deportment.

pairs.display: She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri, they approached slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice calling to them.
pairs.genji_half: She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: they approached slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice calling to them.
pairs.genji_source:
She was told that this was Prince Yūgiri,
Genji’s son by Lady Aoi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They all stopped, and perceived that it was Don Quixote who was making
signals, and shaking off their fears to some extent, they approached
slowly until they were near enough to hear distinctly Don Quixote’s voice
calling to them.

pairs.display: This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had been so near him, and on joining him let himself drop off Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.
pairs.genji_half: This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had been so near him,
pairs.quijote_half: and on joining him let himself drop off Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.
pairs.genji_source:
This she did, while Genji, still unaware that she had
been so near him, spent the rest of the evening preforming his vows
within the Shrine.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, lying across his
ass, followed him, as has been said, and at length came up, having by
this time recovered his senses, and on joining him let himself drop off
Dapple at Rocinante’s feet, sore, bruised, and belaboured.

pairs.display: This could not be denied, Don Quixote sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had promised.
pairs.genji_half: This could not be denied,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had promised.
pairs.genji_source:
This could not
be denied, and indeed to Genji too a joyful time had succeeded to an
age of sadness.
pairs.quijote_source:
The ladies, then, having retired to their chamber, and the others having
disposed themselves with as little discomfort as they could, Don Quixote
sallied out of the inn to act as sentinel of the castle as he had
promised.

pairs.display: Despite their total lack of sense or breeding, and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.
pairs.genji_half: Despite their total lack of sense or breeding,
pairs.quijote_half: and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Despite their
total lack of sense or breeding, their company appeared to afford a
vast deal of satisfaction to the hot-blood young gentlemen who formed
Genji’s escort.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were at once recognised by both the
curate and the bachelor, who came towards them with open arms.

pairs.display: Her answer to Genji was full of reservations is impossible; he would not have told a lie though he were shot to death with arrows.
pairs.genji_half: Her answer to Genji was full of reservations
pairs.quijote_half: is impossible; he would not have told a lie though he were shot to death with arrows.
pairs.genji_source:
Her answer
to Genji was full of reservations and misgivings concerning her fitness
for the position which he promised her.
pairs.quijote_source:
For me to believe that Don Quixote could lie, he being
the most truthful gentleman and the noblest knight of his time, is
impossible; he would not have told a lie though he were shot to death
with arrows.

pairs.display: The retirement of the Emperor Suzaku had necessitated the appointment of a new Vestal at Ise, for him to revoke the mandate and take away the government from him he would have done so.
pairs.genji_half: The retirement of the Emperor Suzaku had necessitated the appointment of a new Vestal at Ise,
pairs.quijote_half: for him to revoke the mandate and take away the government from him he would have done so.
pairs.genji_source:
The retirement of the Emperor Suzaku had necessitated the appointment
of a new Vestal at Ise, and Lady Rokujō had brought her daughter back
again to the City.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is recorded, then, that as soon as Sancho had gone, Don Quixote felt
his loneliness, and had it been possible for him to revoke the mandate
and take away the government from him he would have done so.

pairs.display: Before the funeral Genji called in person upon us in place of the real husband.
pairs.genji_half: Before the funeral Genji called in person
pairs.quijote_half: upon us in place of the real husband.
pairs.genji_source:
Before the funeral Genji called in person
and sent in a note to the bereaved lady of the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
They have put Tosilos, my
lord the duke’s lacquey, upon us in place of the real husband.

pairs.display: The ladies concerned knew that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears, Don Quixote without his armour and in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the armour.
pairs.genji_half: The ladies concerned knew that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote without his armour and in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the armour.
pairs.genji_source:
The ladies concerned knew
that their doings would probably reach Genji’s ears, and they were far
too anxious to stand well with him to dream of abusing their position.
pairs.quijote_source:
So the two of them took their departure, and Don Quixote and Sancho
afterwards, as has been already said, Don Quixote without his armour and
in travelling gear, and Sancho on foot, Dapple being loaded with the
armour.

pairs.display: The fact that Genji frequented the house had and loyal to the Truth that he would not for the world say anything opposed to them.
pairs.genji_half: The fact that Genji frequented the house had
pairs.quijote_half: and loyal to the Truth that he would not for the world say anything opposed to them.
pairs.genji_source:
The fact that Genji frequented the house had for the time being induced
a certain number of other visitors to present themselves.
pairs.quijote_source:
To all this the barber gave his assent, and looked upon it as right and
proper, being persuaded that the curate was so staunch to the Faith and
loyal to the Truth that he would not for the world say anything opposed
to them.

pairs.display: At last came Genji’s pardon and recall, he himself would make a fair copy of it.
pairs.genji_half: At last came Genji’s pardon and recall,
pairs.quijote_half: he himself would make a fair copy of it.
pairs.genji_source:
At last came Genji’s pardon and recall, celebrated in every part of the
kingdom by riotous holiday-making and rejoicing.
pairs.quijote_source:
On
this the curate said if he showed it to him, he himself would make a fair
copy of it.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Genji celebrated the ritual of the Eight Readings, would not go in to witness the battle between her champion and her opponent.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Genji celebrated the ritual of the Eight Readings,
pairs.quijote_half: would not go in to witness the battle between her champion and her opponent.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Genji celebrated the ritual
of the Eight Readings, in memory of his father, the old Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea, seeing how short and slight his attire was, would not
go in to witness the battle between her champion and her opponent.

pairs.display: Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place for all would go well and turn out as he wished.
pairs.genji_half: Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place
pairs.quijote_half: for all would go well and turn out as he wished.
pairs.genji_source:
Just let Prince Genji have a look at the place for
himself; he’ll soon know how things stand!
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea said she fully believed it, and that he need not be uneasy, for
all would go well and turn out as he wished.

pairs.display: I will go to Prince Genji at once as he saw there was but little more left to read.
pairs.genji_half: I will go to Prince Genji at once
pairs.quijote_half: as he saw there was but little more left to read.
pairs.genji_source: I will go to Prince Genji at once and tell him of this.’
pairs.quijote_source:
All therefore being
appeased, the curate was anxious to go on with the novel, as he saw there
was but little more left to read.

pairs.display: Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers and they embraced once more, and renewed their promises.
pairs.genji_half: Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers
pairs.quijote_half: and they embraced once more, and renewed their promises.
pairs.genji_source:
Even so, the ends of Genji’s trousers became dripping
wet before he reached the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate promised to comply with his request
carefully, and they embraced once more, and renewed their promises.

pairs.display: At last, just as she had always predicted, who screened himself as best he could with his buckler, not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour unprotected.
pairs.genji_half: At last, just as she had always predicted,
pairs.quijote_half: who screened himself as best he could with his buckler, not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour unprotected.
pairs.genji_source:
At last, just as she had
always predicted, Genji had come back!
pairs.quijote_source:
The comrades
of the wounded perceiving the plight they were in began from a distance
to shower stones on Don Quixote, who screened himself as best he could
with his buckler, not daring to quit the trough and leave his armour
unprotected.

pairs.display: The mere fact that Genji’s men were at work upon the building a villain and a low-born knight whom, had he received the order of knighthood, he would call to account for his treachery.
pairs.genji_half: The mere fact that Genji’s men were at work upon the building
pairs.quijote_half: a villain and a low-born knight whom, had he received the order of knighthood, he would call to account for his treachery.
pairs.genji_source:
The mere fact that Genji’s men
were at work upon the building at once set the gossips talking and the
most absurd stories were circulated.
pairs.quijote_source:
Still louder shouted Don Quixote,
calling them knaves and traitors, and the lord of the castle, who allowed
knights-errant to be treated in this fashion, a villain and a low-born
knight whom, had he received the order of knighthood, he would call to
account for his treachery.

pairs.display: It was well known in society that Genji and must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.
pairs.genji_half: It was well known in society that Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.
pairs.genji_source:
It was well known in society
that Genji took little interest in the common run of women.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote thanked them for their advice and for the
disposition they showed to do him a favour, and said that for the present
he would not, and must not go to Seville until he had cleared all these
mountains of highwaymen and robbers, of whom report said they were full.

pairs.display: This jungle Genji now ordered to be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared they ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.
pairs.genji_half: This jungle Genji now ordered to be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared
pairs.quijote_half: they ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.
pairs.genji_source:
This jungle Genji now ordered to
be reduced to tolerable dimensions; he had the ponds cleared and
pleasant streams were made to run in and out among the flower-beds.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote and Sancho dismounted, and turning Rocinante
and the ass loose to feed on the grass that was there in abundance, they
ransacked the alforjas, and without any ceremony very peacefully and
sociably master and man made their repast on what they found in them.

pairs.display: She lived for two years more in the old palace, giving as excuse that he could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote than what has been already set forth.
pairs.genji_half: She lived for two years more in the old palace,
pairs.quijote_half: giving as excuse that he could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote than what has been already set forth.
pairs.genji_source:
She lived for two years more in the old palace, at the end of which
time she moved into the new Eastern Wing that Prince Genji had been
building.
pairs.quijote_source:
But it spoils all, that at this point and crisis the author
of the history leaves this battle impending, giving as excuse that he
could find nothing more written about these achievements of Don Quixote
than what has been already set forth.

pairs.display: Vague rumours reached her concerning Genji’s banishment; it was said that he was in disgrace who grasped her tightly by the wrist, and drawing her towards him, while she dared not utter a word, made her sit down on the bed.
pairs.genji_half: Vague rumours reached her concerning Genji’s banishment; it was said that he was in disgrace
pairs.quijote_half: who grasped her tightly by the wrist, and drawing her towards him, while she dared not utter a word, made her sit down on the bed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Asturian, who went all doubled up and in
silence with her hands before her feeling for her lover, encountered the
arms of Don Quixote, who grasped her tightly by the wrist, and drawing
her towards him, while she dared not utter a word, made her sit down on
the bed.

pairs.display: It had at one time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely, as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.
pairs.genji_half: It had at one time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely,
pairs.quijote_half: as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.
pairs.genji_source:
It had at one
time seemed as though Genji’s banishment might last indefinitely,
far longer in any case than Iyo no Suke’s short term of office.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cuadrillero on this let go Don Quixote’s beard, and
went out to look for a light to search for and apprehend the culprits;
but not finding one, as the innkeeper had purposely extinguished the
lantern on retreating to his room, he was compelled to have recourse to
the hearth, where after much time and trouble he lit another lamp.

pairs.display: But in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the Capital were fellows who would not have cared two farthings for Don Quixote, even had he been really one of the knights-errant of the Round Table.
pairs.genji_half: But in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the Capital
pairs.quijote_half: were fellows who would not have cared two farthings for Don Quixote, even had he been really one of the knights-errant of the Round Table.
pairs.genji_source:
But
in the end it so turned out that Genji had already been back in the
Capital for a year when Iyo’s governorship expired.
pairs.quijote_source:
The innkeeper, as
soon as he saw him off, wanted to bar the gate close, but the blanketers
would not agree to it, for they were fellows who would not have cared two
farthings for Don Quixote, even had he been really one of the
knights-errant of the Round Table.

pairs.display: News now came that Genji’s and exchanging such fisticuffs that if Don Quixote had not made peace between them, they would have knocked one another to pieces.
pairs.genji_half: News now came that Genji’s
pairs.quijote_half: and exchanging such fisticuffs that if Don Quixote had not made peace between them, they would have knocked one another to pieces.
pairs.genji_source:
News
now came that Genji’s procession had crossed the Awata Road.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho retorted, and the goatherd rejoined, and the altercation
ended in their seizing each other by the beard, and exchanging such
fisticuffs that if Don Quixote had not made peace between them, they
would have knocked one another to pieces.

pairs.display: As a boy he had been very good-looking hearers were again filled with wonder at the force of Don Quixote’s madness that could run away with this poor man’s reason.
pairs.genji_half: As a boy he had been very good-looking
pairs.quijote_half: hearers were again filled with wonder at the force of Don Quixote’s madness that could run away with this poor man’s reason.
pairs.genji_source:
As a boy he had been very good-looking and Genji had taken a
great fancy to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this Sancho delivered with so much
composure--wiping his nose from time to time--and with so little
common-sense that his two hearers were again filled with wonder at the
force of Don Quixote’s madness that could run away with this poor man’s
reason.

pairs.display: Soon after this Genji sent for the Captain and how this disguise was intended to get him away from the mountain where he then was.
pairs.genji_half: Soon after this Genji sent for the Captain
pairs.quijote_half: and how this disguise was intended to get him away from the mountain where he then was.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon after this Genji sent for the Captain and gave him a letter to
be taken to his sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlady asked them what they wanted these things for, and the curate
told her in a few words about the madness of Don Quixote, and how this
disguise was intended to get him away from the mountain where he then
was.

pairs.display: Somehow or other, in cases of this kind, and they told the curate all that had passed between him and them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.
pairs.genji_half: Somehow or other, in cases of this kind,
pairs.quijote_half: and they told the curate all that had passed between him and them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.
pairs.genji_source:
Somehow or other, in cases of this kind, Genji could never help
imagining what he himself would feel if he were in the same position.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord and landlady immediately came to the conclusion that
the madman was their guest, the balsam man and master of the blanketed
squire, and they told the curate all that had passed between him and
them, not omitting what Sancho had been so silent about.

pairs.display: There was indeed a letter and the gentlewomen had read it, but they very much doubted whether it was fit was discovered in her bosom which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the light of one of the torches.
pairs.genji_half: There was indeed a letter and the gentlewomen had read it, but they very much doubted whether it was fit
pairs.quijote_half: was discovered in her bosom which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the light of one of the torches.
pairs.genji_source:
There was indeed a
letter and the gentlewomen had read it, but they very much doubted
whether it was fit for Genji’s eyes and made no offer to produce it.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were all thrown into confusion by Luscinda’s fainting, and as her
mother was unlacing her to give her air a sealed paper was discovered in
her bosom which Don Fernando seized at once and began to read by the
light of one of the torches.

pairs.display: He was brooding now over his loss, for his presence just yet, so they allowed the others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on foot.
pairs.genji_half: He was brooding now over his loss,
pairs.quijote_half: for his presence just yet, so they allowed the others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on foot.
pairs.genji_source:
He was brooding now over his loss, when Genji
arrived on a visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio lest he
should remind Don Quixote of the quarrel he had with him, and the curate
as there was no necessity for his presence just yet, so they allowed the
others to go on before them, while they themselves followed slowly on
foot.

pairs.display: It was apparent in a moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being discussed, would by no means allow it, but made her rise and embraced her with great courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.
pairs.genji_half: It was apparent in a moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being discussed,
pairs.quijote_half: would by no means allow it, but made her rise and embraced her with great courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.
pairs.genji_source:
It was apparent in a
moment that Suzaku suffered acutely while these subjects were being
discussed, and Genji, ashamed of his unkindness, hastily turned the
conversation to other matters.
pairs.quijote_source:
The distressed damsel strove with much pertinacity to kiss his hands; but
Don Quixote, who was in all things a polished and courteous knight, would
by no means allow it, but made her rise and embraced her with great
courtesy and politeness, and ordered Sancho to look to Rocinante’s
girths, and to arm him without a moment’s delay.

pairs.display: Presently Genji arrived at the Palace as to permit her to recover and enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all laughing again.
pairs.genji_half: Presently Genji arrived at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: as to permit her to recover and enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all laughing again.
pairs.genji_source:
Presently Genji arrived at the Palace and was greatly diverted by the
spectacle of this disorderly and embittered combat.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea
therefore gave her hand, and promised to make him a great lord in her
kingdom, when Heaven should be so good as to permit her to recover and
enjoy it, for which Sancho returned thanks in words that set them all
laughing again.

pairs.display: Not unnaturally he was feeling somewhat bitterly against Prince Genji; but he realized that he had himself, as well in the story itself as in its conciseness, and the resemblance it bore to those of the books of chivalry.
pairs.genji_half: Not unnaturally he was feeling somewhat bitterly against Prince Genji; but he realized that he had himself,
pairs.quijote_half: as well in the story itself as in its conciseness, and the resemblance it bore to those of the books of chivalry.
pairs.genji_source:
Not unnaturally he was feeling
somewhat bitterly against Prince Genji; but he realized that he had
himself, in past days, deserved none too well at his brother’s hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
While the two had been going along conversing in this fashion, the curate
observed to Dorothea that she had shown great cleverness, as well in the
story itself as in its conciseness, and the resemblance it bore to those
of the books of chivalry.

pairs.display: He had received no official summons on this particular occasion, and in dread of his master catching him tripping, for though he knew that Dulcinea was a peasant girl of El Toboso, he had never seen her in all his life.
pairs.genji_half: He had received no official summons on this particular occasion,
pairs.quijote_half: and in dread of his master catching him tripping, for though he knew that Dulcinea was a peasant girl of El Toboso, he had never seen her in all his life.
pairs.genji_source:
He had received no official summons on this particular
occasion, but had in the end yielded to Genji’s entreaties that he
would come and help him in his difficult task.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote drew
up, not a little to the satisfaction of Sancho, for he was by this time
weary of telling so many lies, and in dread of his master catching him
tripping, for though he knew that Dulcinea was a peasant girl of El
Toboso, he had never seen her in all his life.

pairs.display: An additional advantage was given to Genji’s and would have gone to her to say some words of comfort to her, but was prevented by Don Fernando’s grasp which held her fast.
pairs.genji_half: An additional advantage was given to Genji’s
pairs.quijote_half: and would have gone to her to say some words of comfort to her, but was prevented by Don Fernando’s grasp which held her fast.
pairs.genji_source:
An additional advantage was given to Genji’s paintings by the pathos
of the subject.
pairs.quijote_source:
Luscinda stood regarding her with no
less compassion for her sufferings than admiration for her intelligence
and beauty, and would have gone to her to say some words of comfort to
her, but was prevented by Don Fernando’s grasp which held her fast.

pairs.display: The spectators now clamoured only for more specimens of Genji’s handiwork, so he rose from the table and going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda, Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.
pairs.genji_half: The spectators now clamoured only for more specimens of Genji’s handiwork,
pairs.quijote_half: so he rose from the table and going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda, Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.
pairs.genji_source:
The spectators now clamoured only for more
specimens of Genji’s handiwork, and it would have been impossible at
that moment to interest them in anything else.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate, seeing, then, how well he had
succeeded in carrying out his purpose and the captain’s wishes, had no
desire to keep them unhappy any longer, so he rose from the table and
going into the room where Zoraida was he took her by the hand, Luscinda,
Dorothea, and the Judge’s daughter following her.

pairs.display: It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat discursive, came out in dismay, and ran to see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.
pairs.genji_half: It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat discursive,
pairs.quijote_half: came out in dismay, and ran to see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was already nearing the dawn when Genji, feeling somewhat
discursive, sent round the great tankard and presently began telling
stories to the company.
pairs.quijote_source:
So loud, in fact, were the shouts of Don Quixote, that the landlord
opening the gate of the inn in all haste, came out in dismay, and ran to
see who was uttering such cries, and those who were outside joined him.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō took the _wagon_, which he played, and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō took the _wagon_, which he played,
pairs.quijote_half: and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō took the
_wagon_, which he played, if not so well as Genji, at any rate in
a very distinguished manner.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, finding
himself free, strove to get on top of the goatherd, who, with his face
covered with blood, and soundly kicked by Sancho, was on all fours
feeling about for one of the table-knives to take a bloody revenge with.

pairs.display: Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard, suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white like penitents.
pairs.genji_half: Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern
pairs.quijote_half: and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard, suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white like penitents.
pairs.genji_source:
Sochi no Miya took the great-zithern and
Genji the _kin_.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goatherd, who was by this time tired of pummelling and
being pummelled, released him at once, and Don Quixote rising to his feet
and turning his eyes to the quarter where the sound had been heard,
suddenly saw coming down the slope of a hill several men clad in white
like penitents.

pairs.display: Genji gave instructions that the Suma and poured out fresh maledictions on those accursed books of chivalry; all which was renewed when they saw Don Quixote coming in at the gate.
pairs.genji_half: Genji gave instructions that the Suma
pairs.quijote_half: and poured out fresh maledictions on those accursed books of chivalry; all which was renewed when they saw Don Quixote coming in at the gate.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji gave instructions that the Suma scroll should be left with
Fujitsubo.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was
piteous to hear the cries the two good ladies raised, how they beat their
breasts and poured out fresh maledictions on those accursed books of
chivalry; all which was renewed when they saw Don Quixote coming in at
the gate.

pairs.display: Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony Carrasco fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was resumed.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony
pairs.quijote_half: Carrasco fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was resumed.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had a strong presentiment the Court ceremony and festivals of the
reign were destined to be taken as a model in future times.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bachelor accepted the invitation and remained, a couple of young
pigeons were added to the ordinary fare, at dinner they talked chivalry,
Carrasco fell in with his host’s humour, the banquet came to an end, they
took their afternoon sleep, Sancho returned, and their conversation was
resumed.

pairs.display: What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one and thus they bade each other farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for their expedition.
pairs.genji_half: What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one
pairs.quijote_half: and thus they bade each other farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for their expedition.
pairs.genji_source:
What part could she hope to play save the humiliating one of a foil
to the young princess who was Genji’s lawful bride?
pairs.quijote_source:
Carrasco promised all, and then took
his leave, charging Don Quixote to inform him of his good or evil
fortunes whenever he had an opportunity; and thus they bade each other
farewell, and Sancho went away to make the necessary preparations for
their expedition.

pairs.display: Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her so that he could not see his face; he observed, however, that he was a sturdily built man, but not very tall in stature.
pairs.genji_half: Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her
pairs.quijote_half: so that he could not see his face; he observed, however, that he was a sturdily built man, but not very tall in stature.
pairs.genji_source:
Little as she trusted Genji, she must not cut off her child from all
possibility of an ultimate transference to the Capital.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote examined his adversary, and
found that he already had his helmet on and visor lowered, so that he
could not see his face; he observed, however, that he was a sturdily
built man, but not very tall in stature.

pairs.display: And since the spring they have been building this new hermitage and his squire parted from Don Quixote and Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and strap his ribs.
pairs.genji_half: And since the spring they have been building this new hermitage
pairs.quijote_half: and his squire parted from Don Quixote and Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and strap his ribs.
pairs.genji_source:
And since the spring they have been building
this new hermitage for Prince Genji close by, and this has changed the
whole character of the district.
pairs.quijote_source:
In fine, both
master and man remained under the delusion; and, down in the mouth, and
out of luck, he of the Mirrors and his squire parted from Don Quixote and
Sancho, he meaning to go look for some village where he could plaster and
strap his ribs.

pairs.display: He noted that the old priest and squeezed the whey began to run all over his face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:
pairs.genji_half: He noted that the old priest
pairs.quijote_half: and squeezed the whey began to run all over his face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:
pairs.genji_source:
He noted that the old priest was evidently
on friendly terms with Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote took it, and without
perceiving what was in it thrust it down in hot haste upon his head; but
as the curds were pressed and squeezed the whey began to run all over his
face and beard, whereat he was so startled that he cried out to Sancho:

pairs.display: Genji meanwhile had no notion of what was afoot and could not understand why, proceeded to recall the others, who still continued to fly, looking back at every step, all in a body, the gentleman bringing up the rear.
pairs.genji_half: Genji meanwhile had no notion of what was afoot and could not understand why,
pairs.quijote_half: proceeded to recall the others, who still continued to fly, looking back at every step, all in a body, the gentleman bringing up the rear.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji meanwhile had no notion of what was afoot and could not
understand why, after all his entreaties, the Lady of Akashi still hung
back.
pairs.quijote_source:
The keeper obeyed, and Don Quixote, fixing on the point of his lance the
cloth he had wiped his face with after the deluge of curds, proceeded to
recall the others, who still continued to fly, looking back at every
step, all in a body, the gentleman bringing up the rear.

pairs.display: A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told them.
pairs.genji_half: A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived
pairs.quijote_half: but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told them.
pairs.genji_source:
A member of Genji’s personal servants now arrived at Akashi to assist
the family in their removal.
pairs.quijote_source:
The instant Sancho heard
this he steered his Dapple towards it, and Don Quixote and the cousin did
the same; but it seems Sancho’s bad luck so ordered it that the hermit
was not at home, for so a sub-hermit they found in the hermitage told
them.

pairs.display: On their arrival they found that entertainment had been prepared and cousin, accommodated with the best places, the interpreter began to say what he will hear or see who reads or hears the next chapter.
pairs.genji_half: On their arrival they found that entertainment had been prepared
pairs.quijote_half: and cousin, accommodated with the best places, the interpreter began to say what he will hear or see who reads or hears the next chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
On their arrival they
found that entertainment had been prepared for them at Genji’s command
by one of his confidential servants.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so, all who were in the inn being
arranged in front of the show, some of them standing, and Don Quixote,
Sancho, the page, and cousin, accommodated with the best places, the
interpreter began to say what he will hear or see who reads or hears the
next chapter.

pairs.display: At last she took out the zithern not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain a moment separated.
pairs.genji_half: At last she took out the zithern
pairs.quijote_half: not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain a moment separated.
pairs.genji_source:
At last she took out the zithern which Genji
had given to her at Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They put up Sancho,
scarcely restored to his senses, on his ass, and let him go after his
master; not that he was sufficiently in his wits to guide the beast, but
Dapple followed the footsteps of Rocinante, from whom he could not remain
a moment separated.

pairs.display: While things were going thus dismally they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: While things were going thus dismally
pairs.quijote_half: they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
While things were going thus dismally at Ōi, Genji was feeling very
uneasy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote passed it in his never-failing meditations; but, for
all that, they had some winks of sleep, and with the appearance of
daylight they pursued their journey in quest of the banks of the famous
Ebro, where that befell them which will be told in the following chapter.

pairs.display: Every one used to make such a fuss about Yūgiri, and at the far end of it observed some people, and as he drew nearer saw that it was a hawking party.
pairs.genji_half: Every one used to make such a fuss about Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and at the far end of it observed some people, and as he drew nearer saw that it was a hawking party.
pairs.genji_source:
Every one used to make
such a fuss about Yūgiri, Princess Aoi’s child, and pretend it was so
remarkably handsome.
pairs.quijote_source:
It so happened that the next day towards sunset, on coming out of a wood,
Don Quixote cast his eyes over a green meadow, and at the far end of it
observed some people, and as he drew nearer saw that it was a hawking
party.

pairs.display: He had himself formed some slight acquaintance with her during the period of Genji’s exile and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial estate in that suit.
pairs.genji_half: He had himself formed some slight acquaintance with her during the period of Genji’s exile
pairs.quijote_half: and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial estate in that suit.
pairs.genji_source:
He had himself formed some slight acquaintance
with her during the period of Genji’s exile and wished to discover
whether she still had a liking for him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote went over and unhooked Sancho, who, as soon
as he found himself on the ground, looked at the rent in his huntingcoat
and was grieved to the heart, for he thought he had got a patrimonial
estate in that suit.

pairs.display: Two officious gentlemen, the Captain of the Guard but there was no possibility of it until they themselves displayed them of their own accord and free will.
pairs.genji_half: Two officious gentlemen, the Captain of the Guard
pairs.quijote_half: but there was no possibility of it until they themselves displayed them of their own accord and free will.
pairs.genji_source:
Two
officious gentlemen, the Captain of the Guard and a certain Hyoye no
Kami, rode at the back of Genji’s coach.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote remained silent,
while Sancho was dying to see the features of Trifaldi and one or two of
her many duennas; but there was no possibility of it until they
themselves displayed them of their own accord and free will.

pairs.display: Their strange, clipped talk reminded Genji of the fishermen at Suma with windows, gratings, gardens, serenades, lovemakings, and languishings, that he had read of in his trashy books of chivalry, came to his mind.
pairs.genji_half: Their strange, clipped talk reminded Genji of the fishermen at Suma
pairs.quijote_half: with windows, gratings, gardens, serenades, lovemakings, and languishings, that he had read of in his trashy books of chivalry, came to his mind.
pairs.genji_source:
Their strange, clipped talk
reminded Genji of the fishermen at Suma and greatly diverted him.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he
listened to all this Don Quixote was in a state of breathless amazement,
for immediately the countless adventures like this, with windows,
gratings, gardens, serenades, lovemakings, and languishings, that he had
read of in his trashy books of chivalry, came to his mind.

pairs.display: To Genji it suddenly occurred that flew at his face and held on to his nose tooth and nail, with the pain of which he began to shout his loudest.
pairs.genji_half: To Genji it suddenly occurred that
pairs.quijote_half: flew at his face and held on to his nose tooth and nail, with the pain of which he began to shout his loudest.
pairs.genji_source:
To Genji it suddenly occurred that he had never written
the customary letter.
pairs.quijote_source:
They dashed at the grating and
escaped by it, save one that, finding itself hard pressed by the slashes
of Don Quixote’s sword, flew at his face and held on to his nose tooth
and nail, with the pain of which he began to shout his loudest.

pairs.display: But while he was speaking the Lady of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past, bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out, and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.
pairs.genji_half: But while he was speaking the Lady of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past,
pairs.quijote_half: bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out, and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.
pairs.genji_source:
But while he was speaking the Lady
of Akashi recalled the stories of Genji’s adventurous past, and of
numerous other attachments with which rumour credited him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The letters were applauded, laughed over, relished, and admired; and
then, as if to put the seal to the business, the courier arrived,
bringing the one Sancho sent to Don Quixote, and this, too, was read out,
and it raised some doubts as to the governor’s simplicity.

pairs.display: All the time they were driving to the Capital Genji was haunted and when he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.
pairs.genji_half: All the time they were driving to the Capital Genji was haunted
pairs.quijote_half: and when he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.
pairs.genji_source:
All the time they were driving
to the Capital Genji was haunted by the image of the sorrow-stricken
figure that had watched their departure.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here Sancho brought his long speech to an end, Don Quixote having been
the whole time in dread of his uttering a host of absurdities; and when
he found him leave off with so few, he thanked heaven in his heart.

pairs.display: But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon its upbringing, but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give satisfaction.
pairs.genji_half: But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon its upbringing,
pairs.quijote_half: but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give satisfaction.
pairs.genji_source:
But news had reached them of the pains which Genji was bestowing upon
its upbringing, and she felt no doubt that she had advised for the best.
pairs.quijote_source:
The conditions of the combat were that if Don Quixote proved
the victor his antagonist was to marry the daughter of Dona Rodriguez;
but if he should be vanquished his opponent was released from the promise
that was claimed against him and from all obligations to give
satisfaction.

pairs.display: She had her work cut out for her in keeping up to the mark all the writing-women and courtesy enshrined in the nymphs that dwell in these meadows and groves surpass all upon earth, putting aside the lady of my heart, Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: She had her work cut out for her in keeping up to the mark all the writing-women
pairs.quijote_half: and courtesy enshrined in the nymphs that dwell in these meadows and groves surpass all upon earth, putting aside the lady of my heart, Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
She had her work cut out for her in keeping up to the
mark all the writing-women and young girls whom Genji had allotted to
her service.
pairs.quijote_source:
Know that Don Quixote of La Mancha, knight-errant, is posted here
to maintain by arms that the beauty and courtesy enshrined in the nymphs
that dwell in these meadows and groves surpass all upon earth, putting
aside the lady of my heart, Dulcinea del Toboso.

pairs.display: Thus everything seemed to be working very smoothly, and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man, seated themselves.
pairs.genji_half: Thus everything seemed to be working very smoothly,
pairs.quijote_half: and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man, seated themselves.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus everything seemed to be working very
smoothly, and Genji felt that the arrangement was going to be a great
success.
pairs.quijote_source:
A clear limpid spring which they discovered in a cool grove relieved Don
Quixote and Sancho of the dust and fatigue due to the unpolite behaviour
of the bulls, and by the side of this, having turned Dapple and Rocinante
loose without headstall or bridle, the forlorn pair, master and man,
seated themselves.

pairs.display: While speculation thus reigned on every side, had for him, giving special thanks to heaven that this inn had not been taken for a castle by his master.
pairs.genji_half: While speculation thus reigned on every side,
pairs.quijote_half: had for him, giving special thanks to heaven that this inn had not been taken for a castle by his master.
pairs.genji_source:
While speculation thus reigned on every side, Genji held
in his heart a guilty secret which might well be the key to these
distressing portents.
pairs.quijote_source:
He took the beasts to the stable, fed them, and came back to see
what orders Don Quixote, who was seated on a bench at the door, had for
him, giving special thanks to heaven that this inn had not been taken for
a castle by his master.

pairs.display: Learning that his Majesty had not risen, road to Barcelona without touching upon Saragossa; so anxious was he to make out this new historian, who they said abused him so, to be a liar.
pairs.genji_half: Learning that his Majesty had not risen,
pairs.quijote_half: road to Barcelona without touching upon Saragossa; so anxious was he to make out this new historian, who they said abused him so, to be a liar.
pairs.genji_source:
Learning that his Majesty
had not risen, Genji assumed that he was indisposed and at once
called to enquire.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was a fresh morning giving promise of a cool day as Don Quixote
quitted the inn, first of all taking care to ascertain the most direct
road to Barcelona without touching upon Saragossa; so anxious was he to
make out this new historian, who they said abused him so, to be a liar.

pairs.display: During the first weeks of mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.
pairs.genji_half: During the first weeks of mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.
pairs.genji_source:
During the first weeks of
mourning Genji spent all his time at the Palace and paid an early
visit to the Emperor every day.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote was on foot with his horse
unbridled and his lance leaning against a tree, and in short completely
defenceless; he thought it best therefore to fold his arms and bow his
head and reserve himself for a more favourable occasion and opportunity.

pairs.display: Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will not repeat, and Sancho (to whom he presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given), left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.
pairs.genji_half: Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will not repeat,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho (to whom he presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given), left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus Genji managed, by arguments which for fear of wearying you I will
not repeat, to coax the Emperor into a slightly less desperate state of
mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
They reached the strand on Saint John’s Eve during
the night; and Roque, after embracing Don Quixote and Sancho (to whom he
presented the ten crowns he had promised but had not until then given),
left them with many expressions of good-will on both sides.

pairs.display: For years past it had struck the Emperor, and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.
pairs.genji_half: For years past it had struck the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past it had struck the Emperor, on looking at himself
in the mirror, that he was extraordinarily like Prince Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The commandant
had been already made aware of his good fortune in seeing two such famous
persons as Don Quixote and Sancho, and the instant they came to the shore
all the galleys struck their awnings and the clarions rang out.

pairs.display: But if he was Genji’s son, Genji too must be aware of the fact, and in a robe of white taffeta embroidered with gold flowers, her hair flowing loose over her shoulders, and leaning upon a staff of fine black ebony.
pairs.genji_half: But if he was Genji’s son, Genji too must be aware of the fact,
pairs.quijote_half: and in a robe of white taffeta embroidered with gold flowers, her hair flowing loose over her shoulders, and leaning upon a staff of fine black ebony.
pairs.genji_source:
But if he was Genji’s son, Genji too must be aware
of the fact, and it was absurd that the relationship should not be
acknowledged between them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Altisidora, come back from death to life
as Don Quixote fancied, following up the freak of her lord and lady,
entered the chamber, crowned with the garland she had worn on the
catafalque and in a robe of white taffeta embroidered with gold flowers,
her hair flowing loose over her shoulders, and leaning upon a staff of
fine black ebony.

pairs.display: But to Genji, he supposed, and the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_half: But to Genji, he supposed,
pairs.quijote_half: and the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_source:
But to Genji, he supposed, the whole matter
must be a very painful one.
pairs.quijote_source:
They gave it very readily, and the duchess asked him if
Altisidora was in his good graces.

pairs.display: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew, turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_half: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew,
pairs.quijote_half: turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_source:
But he longed, without actually
letting out that he knew, to get some further information from Genji
himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
They dismounted at a hostelry which Don Quixote recognised as such
and did not take to be a castle with moat, turrets, portcullis, and
drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more
rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.

pairs.display: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_half: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_source:
She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder
daughter, and it was entirely in this spirit that Genji entertained
and amused her.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that day Don Quixote and Sancho remained in the village and inn
waiting for night, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the
open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the
accomplishment of his wishes.

pairs.display: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_half: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer,
pairs.quijote_half: and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_source:
So the old poet
wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, for
it seems that in him every perfection is combined.’
pairs.quijote_source:
So at length, with the boys capering round them, and accompanied by the
curate and the bachelor, they made their entrance into the town, and
proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his
housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.

pairs.display: Soon however the extreme stiffness and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.
pairs.genji_half: Soon however the extreme stiffness
pairs.quijote_half: and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon
however the extreme stiffness and formality of address which Genji
henceforth adopted began somewhat to restore her confidence.
pairs.quijote_source:
The notary was there at the time, and he said that in no book
of chivalry had he ever read of any knight-errant dying in his bed so
calmly and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and
lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.

pairs.display: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir
        Apparent, son of the Emperor Ryōzen.
pairs.quijote_source:
Master Pedro now came back, and in a cart followed the show and the
ape--a big one, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not
vicious-looking.

pairs.display: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune, Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_half: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune,
pairs.quijote_half: Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_source:
Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no
        Mototsune, 891 A.D.

    That Ryōzen was in reality Genji’s son.
pairs.quijote_source:
She who would rather see your ladyship than write to you,

Your servant,

TERESA PANZA.

pairs.display: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no mood given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_half: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no mood
pairs.quijote_half: given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_source:
After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no
mood for sleep, and soon he jumped up and threw open his casement.
pairs.quijote_source:
He gave her the acorns, and also a cheese which Teresa had
given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.

pairs.display: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt, and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_half: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt,
pairs.quijote_half: and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past she had held, beyond
challenge or doubt, the first place in Genji’s affections—had been the
centre of all his plans and contrivings.
pairs.quijote_source:
He described to them also nearly all the adventures that Sancho
had mentioned, at which they marvelled and laughed not a little, thinking
it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be
capable of.

pairs.display: Genji, meanwhile, spent much of his time in the front of the house who at a brisk pace, without taking leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach, turned into a wood that was hard by.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, meanwhile, spent much of his time in the front of the house
pairs.quijote_half: who at a brisk pace, without taking leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach, turned into a wood that was hard by.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, meanwhile, spent much of
his time in the front of the house and was also frequently at the
Emperor’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho gave him many thanks, and again kissing his hand and the skirt of
his hauberk, helped him to mount Rocinante, and mounting his ass himself,
proceeded to follow his master, who at a brisk pace, without taking
leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach,
turned into a wood that was hard by.

pairs.display: The words, ordinary enough in themselves, been pleased to bestow upon you, employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may serve to benefit your conscience and add to your honour.
pairs.genji_half: The words, ordinary enough in themselves,
pairs.quijote_half: been pleased to bestow upon you, employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may serve to benefit your conscience and add to your honour.
pairs.genji_source:
The words, ordinary enough in themselves, filled Genji with an
unaccountable depression.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, Señor Don Quixote, have some compassion
for yourself, return to the bosom of common sense, and make use of the
liberal share of it that heaven has been pleased to bestow upon you,
employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may
